https://bitbucket.org/ruamel/yaml is the link to the package's source code repo, it's ruamel.yaml on pypi.

ruamel.yaml is really nice because it can preserve original formatting, meaning comments, and ordering of maps, etc is preserved. If `yq` could support this as an optional dependency and flag that allows controlling usage, that would be great. I'd even potentially look into implementing it if it doesn't seem too hard.

Hi!

I use [django-taggit](https://github.com/alex/django-taggit) in my project but I encounter problems with mixer. When I blend models with the TagManager attached I get the following message:
> AttributeError: Mixer (transactions.Transaction): type object 'TaggableManager' has no attribute '_meta'

When i add **mixer.SKIP** to the field I get another error message:
> AttributeError: Mixer (transactions.Transaction): 'TaggableManager' object has no attribute 'm2m_field_name'

Here's some pseudo code how I use the TaggableManager:


    from taggit.managers import TaggableManager

    class Transaction(models.Model):
        # some standard django fields here
        tags = TaggableManager()

    # I add values like this 
    trx = Transaction()
    trx.tags.add("TagA", "TagB")


Can I skip blending for this field completely or better yet define my own handler? Thank you.

issue_title: String sort on numeric values in python 3
issue_body: Sorting tasks on numeric columns is broken in python 3, in that the sort is done via string comparison, not numeric comparison; this bug was introduced in a86bf0478f23f5834b72c256ee0ce0afc4199fed.  This causes `1000` to come before `2`.

I could use some clarification on what format I should supply the eta option to the API. Is this currently possible? 

I'm get a 500 Internal Server when I attempt to include the eta option (this works fine without it).

```
curl -v -X POST --data '{"args":[1,524],"eta":"2015-03-11T14:08:06-04:00"}' http://localhost:5555/api/task/async-apply/tasks.add                                                  
```

```
[E 150309 19:24:53 web:1421] Uncaught exception POST /api/task/async-apply/tasks.add (10.0.2.2)
    HTTPServerRequest(protocol='http', host='localhost:5555', method='POST', uri='/api/task/async-apply/tasks.add', version='HTTP/1.1', remote_ip='10.0.2.2', headers={'Content-Length': '50', 'Host': 'localhost:5555', 'User-Agent': 'curl/7.22.0 (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu) libcurl/7.22.0 OpenSSL/1.0.1 zlib/1.2.3.4 libidn/1.23 librtmp/2.3', 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded', 'Accept': '*/*'})
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/tornado/web.py", line 1346, in _execute
        result = method(*self.path_args, **self.path_kwargs)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/tornado/web.py", line 2626, in wrapper
        return method(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/flower/api/tasks.py", line 106, in post
        result = task.apply_async(args=args, kwargs=kwargs, **options)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/celery/app/task.py", line 555, in apply_async
        **dict(self._get_exec_options(), **options)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/celery/app/base.py", line 353, in send_task
        reply_to=reply_to or self.oid, **options
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/celery/app/amqp.py", line 265, in publish_task
        eta = eta and eta.isoformat()
    AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'isoformat'

```

Investigating the issue I found that this is a problem with how flower manages to get time. Here is what I think should be changed:

The generation of the *lastquery* timestamp is coming from javascript, that means that if the user computer has a problem on his clock he will not be able to see tasks coming and others will (making the bug hunting a pain). This could be avoided by using python's **time** library and adding a *padding* to that time instead which will use the server time. We could also add a url query like *lastquery* to modify the padding if required.

Here is a code example:

```
time_padding = self.get_argument('padding', type=float)
timestamp = time.time() - padding # let's say padding is 60 seconds
state = self.application.events.state

data = defaultdict(int)
for _, task in state.itertasks():
    if (timestamp < task.timestamp and task.state == states.SUCCESS):
        data[task.worker.hostname] += 1
```

Also, to be honest, I don't quite understand why these timestamp comparisons on the monitor view even exists, for what I could see, the tasks are sent to the monitor just once, even if the times are not exactly matching, shouldn't we just show them?

I understand this may be the case with RabbitMQ but not with other brokers and that would make sense. I think a padding to the time would make things a lot easier to handle though.

I am using Celery with : 
CELERY_ACCEPT_CONTENT = ['application/json']
CELERY_TASK_SERIALIZER = 'json'
CELERY_RESULT_SERIALIZER = 'json'

I added this into flower_config.py but it seems it is still serializing task with pickle, since my worker tells me : 
```
body: '\x80\x02}q\x01(U\x07expiresq\x02NU\x03utcq\x03\x88U\x04argsq\x04]q\x05U\x05chordq\x06NU\tcallbacksq\x07NU\x08errbacksq\x08NU\x07tasksetq\tNU\x02idq\nU$c9929e37-85ee-4cca-8cb7-8ba325a0e282q\x0bU\x07retriesq\x0cK\x00U\x04taskq\rU1gopher_rocon_bootstrap.celery_tasks.delivery_rappq\x0eU\ttimelimitq\x0fNN\x86U\x03etaq\x10NU\x06kwargsq\x11}q\x12u.' (245b)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/alexv/ROS/gopher_rocon/src/flask-ext-catkin/src/kombu/messaging.py", line 590, in _receive_callback
    decoded = None if on_m else message.decode()
  File "/home/alexv/ROS/gopher_rocon/src/flask-ext-catkin/src/kombu/message.py", line 142, in decode
    self.content_encoding, accept=self.accept)
  File "/home/alexv/ROS/gopher_rocon/src/flask-ext-catkin/src/kombu/serialization.py", line 174, in loads
    raise self._for_untrusted_content(content_type, 'untrusted')
ContentDisallowed: Refusing to deserialize untrusted content of type pickle (application/x-python-serialize)
```

So it seems my configuration is ignored... and I cannot find the place where the flowerconfig.py contents are passed to the celery app...

Any hints ? any other way to use json instead ?

issue_title: Refresh interfaces available on a DBus object
issue_body:
Over at Osmocom.org we're using ofono to interact with modems. ofono has the peculiarity that a modem represented by a DBus path may have new DBus interfaces appearing or existing ones disappearing e.g. by setting their 'Powered' to 'true' / 'false'. It works like this:

- There is a property 'Interfaces'.
- I connect to a signal 'PropertyChanged'
- When I tell a modem to get busy, I typically get a 'PropertyChanged' fired, with the list of DBus interfaces that have now magically become available.

Here is my problem: when I receive that signal and I ask my pydbus object instance that represents the modem for this interface, I get a KeyError.

```
   File "/home/neels/tester/src/osmo_gsm_tester/ofono_client.py", line 113, in build_imsi_modem_map
    sim_prop = dbus_obj[I_SIM].GetProperties()
   File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/pydbus/proxy.py", line 105, in __getitem__
    raise KeyError(iface)
 KeyError: 'org.ofono.SimManager'
```


I dug up pydbus code and noticed that of course, the proxy object is composed from evaluating the Introspection XML. It appears that there is no way to update this list of available interfaces once the object is created: in proxy.py, a new class is defined on-the-fly and instantiated. To benefit from changes in the Introspection information, pydbus needs to define a whole new CompositeObject class and instantiate it. Right?

Simple enough, I can modify my code to fetch a new pydbus object every time this happens. But here's what's annoying: I have already connected signals to this object. When I fetch a new object, I have to cancel all my signal subscriptions and establish new ones. Right?

Added annoyance is that each single new interface is announced in a separate signal. So when N new interfaces come up due to modem power on, I disconnect all and re-connect the signals N times over, in a short time.

My dream would be to have a simple my_proxy.refresh() with signal subscriptions as well as the pydbus proxy object identity remaining valid. What is your stance on this? Thanks!

https://git.osmocom.org/osmo-gsm-tester/tree/src/osmo_gsm_tester/ofono_client.py
https://osmocom.org/issues/2233

issue_title: Who runs the finalization of an async generator?
issue_body:
So, I've been looking at Curio's interaction with async-generators.   This is a new feature of Python 3.6.  For example::

    async def countdown(n):
           try:
                while n > 0:
                       yield n
                       n -= 1
           finally:
                print('Countdown done. Yawn!')
                await sleep(1)
                print('Goodbye!')

    async def main():
        async for n in countdown(10):
               print('T-minus', n)

    run(main())

Okay, it's a bit whimsical, but it all works out fine. At the conclusion of the `async-for` loop, you see the code in the `finally:` block run.  You get output like this::

    T-minus 10
    T-minus 9
    T-minus 8
    T-minus 7
    T-minus 6
    T-minus 5
    T-minus 4
    T-minus 3
    T-minus 2
    T-minus 1
    Countdown done. Yawn!
    Goodbye!

That's all well and good until something bad happens.  For example, maybe the for-loop gets prematurely terminated::

    async def main():
        async for n in countdown(10):
               print('T-minus', n)
               if n < 5:
                   print('Abort!')
                   break

Now, when you run the code, you get this output:

    T-minus 10
    T-minus 9
    T-minus 8
    T-minus 7
    T-minus 6
    T-minus 5
    T-minus 4
    Abort!
    Countdown done. Yawn!
    Exception ignored in: <async_generator object countdown at 0x1014e2ba8>
    RuntimeError: async generator ignored GeneratorExit
    
Without getting too bogged down in the details of PEP-525, the gist of the issue is when an async-generator gets garbage collected, something (namely the event loop) needs to take responsibility for finalizing it--basically running it through the steps needed to make it terminate and handling all of the async-related calls that might take place as a result (e.g., that embedded `sleep()` call).

There is a hook to plug into all of this and I can make it work with Curio.  That's not the issue. My question concerns the exact run-time context in which this finalization might occur.   

For example, one approach is to hand over all generator finalization to a separate task (maybe the in-kernel loopback task) that simply deals with it as needed.  Disadvantage is that code that finalizes a generator would execute in a different task than the one that was running when the generator was initiated.  So, that would be potentially kind of weird.  Especially if you were making use of task-local storage or some other task-specific context.

Another option is to run the finalization under the umbrella of the creating task. It's a bit tricky, but Curio could certainly track async generators and make sure that they are finalized in the same context in which they were originally initiated.  Tasks could also explicitly cancel all pending async generators if they're about to be cancelled and cleaned up.

Anyways, I'm going to implement *something* to deal with this as a starting point.  I doubt it will be the ending point, but I'd be interested in getting thoughts about all of this.

async def serial_coro():
	await a()
	await b()
	await c()

	pass

Every time we work on a issue, we can add docstrings to the module at the same time. Hope is that eventually we will have good coverage..

issue_title: IncrementalEndpoint start_time arg for timezone aware datetime
issue_body:
When providing a timezone aware datetime for `start_time` to `IncrementalEndpoint`, an incorrect value is sent to Zendesk when the systems local timezone does not match the datetime's timezone. This is highlighted in http://stackoverflow.com/a/11743262 and J.F. Sebastian's comment there.

Line affected: https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/1.1.7/zenpy/lib/endpoint.py#L120

Demo of problem
```python
import pytz
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# create a shared reference point
start_time = datetime.now()

# by assigning timezone, its actually provides a different meaning to the time
utc_start_time = start_time.replace(tzinfo=pytz.timezone('UTC'))
eastern_start_time = start_time.replace(tzinfo=pytz.timezone('US/Eastern'))
assert utc_start_time != eastern_start_time  # passes
assert (utc_start_time - eastern_start_time) != timedelta(0)

# NOTE: my system timezone is US/Eastern, so the epoch_eastern_start_time is actually the correct value
epoch_utc_start_time = utc_start_time.strftime("%s")
epoch_eastern_start_time = eastern_start_time.strftime("%s")
assert epoch_utc_start_time != epoch_eastern_start_time  # fails
```

Workaround: Calculate `start_time` yourself correctly such that a datetime object isn't provided

The connection to production and test environments are using unverified SSL connections. This means a MITM attack can easily take over traffic, despite SSL.

Offending code resides in the [RequestsTransport class](https://github.com/hbldh/pybankid/blob/master/bankid/client.py#L172-203). `Verify` should not be **False**.
```python
    def open(self, request):
        """Fetches the WSDL specification using certificates."""
        self.addcredentials(request)
        resp = requests.get(request.url, data=request.message,
                            headers=request.headers,
                            cert=self.cert, verify=False)
        result = StringIO.StringIO(resp.content.decode('utf-8'))
        return result

    def send(self, request):
        """Posts to SOAP service using certificates."""
        self.addcredentials(request)
        resp = requests.post(request.url, data=request.message,
                             headers=request.headers,
                             cert=self.cert, verify=False)
        result = Reply(resp.status_code, resp.headers, resp.content)
        return result
```

issue_title: allow Semantic Versioning rather that StrictVer in get_current_version
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Reece Hart (BitBucket: [reece](http://bitbucket.org/reece), GitHub: [reece](http://github.com/reece))**

----------------------------------------

It'd be nice to encourage a standard for versioning that is beyond just Python's StrictVersion. 

Semantic Versioning (http://semver.org/) is an obvious option. In addition to specifying version ordering rules, that spec also provides guidelines for what the version mean and when to bump versions.

I'm unsure how this will interact with setuptools.

Solution:

First, edit the .spec file: the hiddenimport should look like this:

```
hiddenimports = ['keyring.backends.file',
                 'keyring.backends.Gnome',
                 'keyring.backends.Google',
                 'keyring.backends.keyczar',
                 'keyring.backends.kwallet',
                 'keyring.backends.multi',
                 'keyring.backends.OS_X',
                 'keyring.backends.pyfs',
                 'keyring.backends.SecretService',
                 'keyring.backends.Windows']
```

If you compile right now, the compiled binary will use `keyring.backends.file` as the keyring manager. You might want to use the Gnome keyring manager, like I did. But even when you force the keyring to do so (using `keyring.set_keyring(keyring.backends.Gnome.Keyring())`), it will not work, because there's something wrong with `Gnome.py`. To solve this problem: edit and put this at the top of `/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/keyring/backends/Gnome.py` :

    import datetime
    from gi.repository import GnomeKeyring

Save and try to compile again. It should work now.

It seems that medium to large projects cause problems on RTD.

- [ ] Stop colorizing output on RTD, they don't let you have color (:rage:)
- [ ] Suppress doxygen output with `/dev/null` on RTD

The hacky `silent` branch verified that [exhale is the cause of `cat conf.py` being the last step before failure](https://github.com/rtfd/readthedocs.org/issues/3580).

If anybody ever reads this, one way to test `exhale` is generate the full api and track it on git (you can force-push this away, or do it on a different branch altogether and kill that branch).

Suppose you got

- docs/
    - api/
        - library_root.rst and all the others
    - doxyoutput/
    - conf.py

Then you want to locally `make html` once.  Now `git add -Af api/ doxyoutput` so that all the generated reStructuredText and doxygen files are going to be available in a `clone`.  Now edit `conf.py` and comment out the `exhale` entry in `extensions` and anything else `exhale` related (don't forget to `git add conf.py` changes).

Hopefully you shouldn't need to do this, but it can help identify where the problem is.  In this case, doing that produced a successful build, so clearly `exhale` is the problem!

issue_title: Fails if numbers are used as keys

issue_body:
I've been using the stop-gap fix in `v0.9.12rc1` to treat `CONTINUE` keys like comments (#117 and #119). So far things have been working well (over ~100k images processed). However, I just hit this error:
```
ValueError: bad card 'CONTINUE= '        '           /   '&' / Current observing orogram'.  CONTINUE not supported
```

Below is a snippet of the offending header:
```
CAMSHUT = 'Open    '           / Camera shutter at exposure start               
PROGRAM = 'Catching Supernovae in the Act with KEGS (Kepler Extra-Galactic &'   
CONTINUE= '        '           /   '&' / Current observing orogram              
OBSERVER= 'DJJ     '           / Observer name(s)                               
```

Something is amiss here. The [FITS convention](https://fits.gsfc.nasa.gov/registry/continue_keyword.html) states that keywords should "have the name  CONTINUE in bytes 1 through 8, and have space characters in bytes 9 and 10" (i.e., no "="). The header above doesn't obey this convention, and thus can be considered corrupt.

Now, we could just claim this is a corrupt header and wash our hands of the matter. However, I'm pretty sure that this header got corrupted by being processed by an older version of `fitsio` that was not treating `CONTINUE` cards correctly. The original header before processing:
```
CAMSHUT = 'Open    '           / Camera shutter at exposure start
PROGRAM = 'Catching Supernovae in the Act with KEGS (Kepler Extra-Galactic &'
CONTINUE  'Survey) and DECam&'
CONTINUE  '&' / Current observing orogram
OBSERVER= 'DJJ     '           / Observer name(s)
```

This type of corruption is consistent with how older versions of `fitsio` dealt with `CONTINUE` cards. Even if the corrupted header does not strictly conform to the FITS convention, it would be nice if we could at least read it with newer versions of `fitsio`.

The change would be relatively minor, just adding `or front=='CONTINU'` [here](https://github.com/esheldon/fitsio/blob/cf0cfec03b9971eeb733a7e85ddb16ab0a0bb015/fitsio/fitslib.py#L4385). However, this would break with the standard convention, which states that CONTINUE will not be followed by "=".

Thoughts from @esheldon?

issue_title: Existing image data not overwritten if different shape
issue_body:
Hi, I've been trying to replace the data in an image HDU with new values.
In the documentation for `fitsio.fitslib.ImageHDU.write` it states that 

> If data already exist in this HDU, they will be overwritten

This works if the new image data is the same shape as the old image data, but
not if they are different. The code below shows an example where this is the
case. Perhaps a call to `fits_resize_img` could be done conditionally in
`PyFITSObject_write_image`? I'm happy to look into this if I get time.

Thanks,
Simon

```python
import fitsio
import numpy as np

initial_data = np.zeros((5, 5))
replacement_data = np.ones((6, 6))

# Sanity check
assert replacement_data.shape != initial_data.shape

# Write the initial data and let fitsio flush to disc
with fitsio.FITS('output.fits', 'rw', clobber=True) as outfile:
    outfile.write(initial_data)

# Update the image data, and flush to disc. As quoted in the source:
#  "If data already exist in this HDU, they will be overwritten"
# https://github.com/esheldon/fitsio/blob/20dc93976a75116d74b2c98896cbbc4ff3bfdefd/fitsio/fitslib.py#L2904
with fitsio.FITS('output.fits', 'rw') as outfile:
    outfile[0].write(replacement_data)

with fitsio.FITS('output.fits') as infile:
    image_shape = infile[0].get_info()['dims']

    # Passing this test means at least the first element was overwritten
    assert infile[0],read()[0][0] == replacement_data[0][0]

    # Check the image shape. A failed assertion means the new array shape is
    # different
    expected = list(map(long, replacement_data.shape))
    assert image_shape == expected, (
        'image shape: %s, expected: %s' % (image_shape, expected)
    )
```

issue_title: Provide error data in exception instance
issue_body:
Many people have reported issues about getting more out of an error. Some wanted to know the key when an error in a dictionary occurred, others wanted the value for which a validation failed. Some of this has been addressed but I think it can be done even better.

I think the key and value should be obtainable from the exception instance as a data structure not only as a string.

For example:
```python
from schema import Schema, SchemaError

x = Schema({"foo": int, "bar": str})
try:
    x.validate({"foo": "lol", "bar": 1})
except SchemaError as e:
    print("args: %s" % e.args)
    print("autos: %s" % e.autos)
    print("code: %s" % e.code)
    print("errors: %s" % e.errors)
```
... gives me ...
```
args: Key 'bar' error:
1 should be instance of 'str'
autos: ["Key 'bar' error:", "1 should be instance of 'str'"]
code: Key 'bar' error:
1 should be instance of 'str'
errors: [None, None]
```
Something like `SchemaError.data` would be super useful:
```python
e.data == {
    "key": "foo",
    "value": "lol"
}
```

issue_title: Instantiation not connected with invocation of _init_
issue_body:
When you ask jedi to find where a class's `_init_` is used, it finds explicit invocations and the definition.  However, it does not find class instantiation using the standard (function) syntax.

For example it does not report the 5th line is a use of `__init__`:

```python
class Claz:
    def __init__(self):
        print("Hello")

Claz()
```

Issue discovered using Visual Studio Code, details in situ at DonJayamanne/pythonVSCode#498.  Jedi 0.9.0 appears to be the version in the VS Code extension.  Python 2.7.12

issue_title: Easier method of overriding color schemes
issue_body: Sometimes you're lumped with a wallpaper that only uses two colours, say, and that's always been a weak point of `wal`. In that case, I've got a suggestion - a flag that defines only the foreground and background colors. Predefined color schemes such as the ones available on terminal.sexy would fill the rest of the colors.

issue_title: restarting the bot via trigger-command, trigger command not marked read on restart, results in loop
issue_body:
i implemented a "/restart" trigger. my goal is to make changes to my code and let the bot restart itself via telegram message.
if triggered this gets called:
```python
def restart_me():
    python = sys.executable
    bot.sendMessage(my_chat_id, "```\nrestarting...\n```", parse_mode="Markdown")
    os.execl(python, python, * sys.argv)
    return 0
```
in the console i notice the expecting result at first, the bot receives the message and starts a new instance. however, the first message the bot gets is the last message **again** - therefore, it keeps restarting itself in a loop. it seems like the last message wasn't marked read by telepot. any ideas on an approach on this?

 $ python3 -V
Python 3.4.2

Version: 10.5

issue_title: What happens with Heroku and multiple instances?
issue_body:
Hi, I read that when you run code in Heroku, it can go to any number of machines and have multiple instances of the code on different machines serving different users. What happens with webhooks then? I'm just a hobby developer, so I don't really know much about webhooks and connections... But wouldn't there be errors like when I try to run 2 local instances of telepot? Or if it doesn't raises errors, how could that affect speed of receiving / sending messages?

PS: my code has

    bot.setWebhook() # unset if was set previously
    bot.setWebhook(URL + SECRET)

And I think that should take care of things, but if feels like there would still be a "tug of war" where each instance is trying to get its webhook back every second

I'm using telepot to run a bot on Raspberry Pi that controls and reports heating in my house.
Issue is that internet connection is not reliable there and sometimes it goes offline (and downtime could be hours). Telepot seems to handle these cases pretty well, but the moment internet goes off it starts infinitely retrying to connect, causing high CPU load. Which is not good as far as other tasks are also running in the background and there are no real reason to do these attempts without timeout in my case.
Is there is a way to configure retry attempts to have 1..5 seconds delay between attempts?
Would be great to have this delay to be an option for **bot.message_loop()**

It appears that this same bug exists upstream as well in Python 3.5.1.

Add global review.

Related: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/issues/381

issue_title: Hangs/dst not set when trying to add a ipv6 route
issue_body:
I have the following snippet:

~~~python
import pyroute2


def add_route(dst, gateway):
    route_key = {'dst': dst, 'gateway': gateway}

    with pyroute2.IPDB() as ipdb:
        print("Add route...")
        ipdb.routes.add(route_key).commit()
        assert ipdb.routes[route_key]


add_route('fd00:6d:3d1a::1/64', '2001:4c8:1023:108::40')
add_route('172.31.0.0/24', '10.10.8.40')
~~~

Works for any ipv4 addresses I set, but hangs with ipv6 with the transactional stuff eventually complaining that "target dst is not set".

I spend some time looking under the hood, and as far as I can tell, the routes stuff is picking up the new route from netlinks, parsing it properly, and doing set item for the "dst" key correctly.

issue_title: Windows sets the properties on both stdout and stderr regardless of the parameter passed
issue_body:
Run demo04.py and instead of seeing text: green/red/green you will see green/red/red.

This means that when stderr changes the color to red, any further stdout output will be red too, instead of reusing the last stdout color (green).

Reported by google code user sorin.sbarnea, Dec 2, 2011
Migrated from https://code.google.com/p/colorama/issues/detail?id=33

issue_title: Escape sequences are unprocessed on Windows with TERM set
issue_body:
On Windows, when the environment variable `TERM` is set to any value, escape sequences will be printed unprocessed (no colored output). Concerning the values of `TERM`/the terminals where this happens, I'm still waiting for user feedback (see msiemens/PyGitUp#24).

According to `git bisect` this behaviour has been introduced in 961428e6c1c3a6855da5dedccfc418f295b54c25.

issue_title: Support for Windows 10 built-in ANSI cmd
issue_body:
Windows 10 has built-in support for ANSI cmd. The ANSI codes should not be stripped.
The module should check for this. I know how to, but I don't know where to put this check..

issue_title: ffmpeg/avconv was not found even though avconv is in PATH
issue_body:
I'm running on Ubuntu 14.04 on a VPS.
When I request a song, it comes up with the error:

issue_title: Failures in struct packing should fail and not hang forever
issue_body:
In #1300 a float is being passed where the struct requires an int. When I debugged, `_pack()` is correctly raising an error when it tries to serialize the request into a struct. However, the foreground thread just hangs when this happens.

This doesn't make sense to me--it's a client side error, so we should be able to fail loudly to the user. The error may be slightly cryptic because all the struct knows is that something is incorrect, it has no idea of the broader context. But at least it'd give the user something to go on...

Alternatively, we could fail silently and mark that particular message as failing and move onto the next message. I personally prefer to blow up loudly, but that's just me. 

This behavior may also apply to `_unpack()`.

See also #1318

issue_title: Perfdata line has one too many ; (semi-colons) per datasource
issue_body:
In the performance data that is output, each datasource has one too many semi-colons.

This:
```
cluster_nodes=2;;;;;
```
Should be:
```
cluster_nodes=2;;;;
```

Some programs discard the performance data because of the last semi-colon. 
The official nagios plugin development guidelines define performance data as:
https://nagios-plugins.org/doc/guidelines.html#AEN200
```
'label'=value[UOM];[warn];[crit];[min];[max]
```

You simply need to change line 295 from:
```
return ("%s=%s%s;%s;%s;%s;%s;" %
```

issue_title: Error on os.remove if user is changed
issue_body:
If a script is launched as root and it calls `Daemonize(... user='notroot'... )` to execute the daemon with non-root permissions, then .pid file will be created by as owned by root since that happens early in setup.

But when the daemon exits, an exception occurs because the nonroot user attempts to delete the root-owned file.

I believe correct behavior would be to change ownership of the .pid file to the daemon user prior to the daemon giving up its root privileges.

Stack trace
```
KeyboardInterrupt
Stopping daemon.
Error in atexit._run_exitfuncs:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/atexit.py", line 24, in _run_exitfuncs
    func(*targs, **kargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/daemonize.py", line 71, in exit
    os.remove(self.pid)
OSError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/var/run/test.pid'
```

I'm not 100% how this would be done and my Google searches have been fruitless.

The latest version of my reauthenticate function was:

async def refreshAuth():

Which, obviously to those more knowledgeable than me, did not work.  I have tried several combinations of this and just calling `user = auth.refresh(user['refreshToken'])` did not help either.

I initialize pyrebase using:

So I've got a lot of reads for BC's 1 through 6, however only one for barcode 7 and I think that caused the crash.

issue_title: When there are multiple optional positional arguments, their completers are combined.
issue_body:
The title is fairly self-explanatory. If you add multiple positional arguments with `nargs='?'`, the completer for every unentered argument is called and combined, rather than just the first unentered one. As far as I can tell, this is not the behavior accepted by argparse (which always assigns optional arguments greedily in positional order). This is easiest to demonstrate by creating multiple arguments with different 'choices' lists.

This issue seems to be somewhat acknowledged by [this comment](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/master/argcomplete/__init__.py#L387), but I don't see where the ambiguity comes into play thanks to the positional ordering. I'm dealing with a choices argument followed by a filename argument, and there's no case where the file completion should need to occur if the choice hasn't been entered yet.

I'm not sure if this is a global issue or just applying to my code, so bear with me...

I have a script that receives a list of subnets and ports to apply to an iptable chain. The chain is then referenced in FORWARD, and the user is able to use the services he/she was supposed to get...

The flow is like:

a. create new chain. Say: **tun0_lmondesi**
b. hook to FORWARD. Say: src and dst references to **tun0_lmondesi**. Example ```iptables -nL|grep lmondesi
tun0_lmondesi  all  --  0.0.0.0/0            10.x.x.43        
tun0_lmondesi  all  --  10.x.x.43         0.0.0.0/0           
Chain tun0_lmondesi (2 references)
...
iptables -nL tun0_lmondesi|wc -l
128
```
c. use insert_rule() to insert rules to the chain

I expect all rules to be available in the new chain (tun0_lmondesi) but **the very first rule inserted is always missing**. The number of rules should've been 129, but it's always 128.

I've added plenty of logging before/after the insert of the rule, so I can see that it gets inserted. However, when the script is done the rule is no longer there! Note that as rules are inserted they get pushed down the chain as insert_rule() does that (and we are not appending). So, the very last rule from iptables -nL tun0_lmondesi should match the first rule from the array of subnets+ports.

If I add a dummy subnet to the array of subnets, the rule is added but the dummy is missing. If I run python interactively, I'm able to type all the same statements and the rule is added.

I also thought this was related to autocommit, so I turned off autocommit everywhere in the script, but still the rule does not show in **iptables -nL** even though it was supposedly inserted by python-iptables.

This has bugged me for days and I can't find an answer. I went from 0.4.0 to 0.10.0 trying to see if the problem was in python-iptables or my code.

Am I doing something wrong?

issue_title: Extra dependencies on raspbian Jessie (raspberry pi)
issue_body:
It works on raspberry pi (raspbian Jessie). Just make sure the following python packages are installed:

```
sudo apt-get install python-dev
sudo pip install netifaces
sudo pip install enum34
```

It might help someone else in the future. :-)

issue_title: Question about Windows Indexing XlsxWriter Created Files
issue_body:
We're using xlsxwriter on a Centos server to create and mail out xlsx workbooks - all works well and the workbooks can be opened by the recipient. We've found though that Windows doesn't seem to index the files so the contents are not searchable through Windows Explorer or Outlook.

Opening the file in Excel and immediately saving it results in a file size change as Excel creates an additional sharedStrings.xml (amongst other changes) and the file contents will now be found in a Windows search. 

Just wondering if anyone else has experienced this and found a workaround, or if this is something that XlsxWriter could even influence.

issue_title: Issue with relative imports when using with Autodesk Fusion 360 plugin
issue_body:
Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter write xlsx files out from Autodesk Fusion 360 addin.  Their addin environment requires libraries to be located within the project folder (they suggest copying libraries from the standard packages folder to your project folder).  When I do that with XlsxWriter, imports explicitly referencing xlsxwriter (in Workbook,py and Packager.py) cause an error.

I can work around it by removing the xlsxwriter name from those imports after downloading/installing from pip, e.g. "from .app import App", but it would be nice if this were changed in the core library (so I don't have to modify the library after installing it).

issue_title: Implement format inheritance, similar to CSS (or Excel actually)
issue_body:
It would be extremely nice if we could apply multiple formats to a cell and have all the format attributes from each format object applied. An example use case would be if you wanted to highlight a column, and then bold certain cells in the column. Right now you have to make sure that the cell format contains all the attributes of the column format or the defaults from the cell format will override those in the column format.

If other people want this I may try to write it myself. Could anyone comment on how the existing formatting architecture would need to be changed?

issue_title: querying database seems to be very slow
issue_body:
From a python scrypt we query `method=blockchain.address.get_balance` on a synched electrumx database and after some stress tests the best throughput we can achieve is about 30 requests / minute

The script is not a fault as if we just make it echo back a simple message without querying the database we can achieve a throughput of about 2500 requests / minute 

I understand from the docs that electrumx is single threaded so I guess this is the bottleneck 

Is there any way to have a better performance or do you think we can query the levedb directly asynchronously at least for balances

any pointers ?

issue_title: zookeeper support via initialization inside pydrill
issue_body:
Hi Wojtek,

I was just checking out your pydrill module and it looks great -- I'd love to use it in a project I'm working on.  One question though, I didn't see any documentation about connecting to a Zookeeper quorum rather than a specific drillbit.  Is this currently supported?  

Thanks,
Dan


---

Hello!

Currently pyDrill doesn’t support zookeeper, it requires you to first use zookeeper bindings [1] to determine which bits are running and connect to one of them.
State is shared across all bits with zookeeper, so any change related to settings will take affect.

I can add support for zookeeper so that before query i would ask ip of bits connected to quorum.

Here is example how to determine ip’s of bits.
import zc.zk
zk = zc.zk.ZooKeeper('127.0.0.1:2181')
zk.get_children('/drill’)   #  [u'sys.options', u'running', u'sys.storage_plugins', u'drillbit1’]
zk.get_children('/drill/drillbit1’) # [u'faa0c8a3-b569-4280-bf04-53f4b76c93e4’, u'aacaf088-d72a-4b7f-ae34-f15fad2cddef’]


PYDRILL_ZOOKEEPER = ['127.0.0.1:2181’, ‚127.0.0.2:2181’]
I think it could be supported by env variable or parameter used to initialize pyDrill.

Please share your ideas so that i can enhance pyDrill to support your needs.

---

Thanks for your reply, the zc code you've provided is very helpful.

I think zookeeper connectivity would be a great addition to pydrill, as connecting to a single node isn't particularly suitable for a production environment.  Perhaps allowing the pydrill.client.PyDrill class to take keyword arguments similar to the JDBC/ODBC drivers, something like (ConnectionType='ZooKeeper', ZKQuorum='<Server1:Port1>,<Server2:Port2>', ZKClusterID= '<Cluster Name>') would be nice.

The APScheduler produces a warning:
`/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pymysql/cursors.py:165: Warning: (1300, u"Invalid utf8 character string: '80027D'")` trying to execute SQL query `UPDATE job_store SET next_run_time=1522694809.97494, job_state='...' WHERE job_store.id = '...'`

I am using MySQL 5.7.17

issue_title: Key error after removing cron task
issue_body:
We removed certain cron_tasks and restarted our rpqueue server. The tasks remained scheduled and when their time came, a KeyError arose in `_get_work()` during the following `if` branch:
```python
if item_id == work[1]:
    # periodic or cron task, reschedule it
    sch = work[-1] if item_id in NEVER_SKIP else time.time()
    delay = REGISTRY[item_id].next()  # KeyError here
```

All of our worker threads died as a result of this KeyError.

In principle this can be avoided, but we had no way to predict it nor reason to expect it. It seems to me a warning rather than a full exception is a more appropriate response. Other parts of `rpqueue` server code do check for the function name before acting on it, so it makes sense to do so here as well.

PR coming shortly.

accept-encodings = latin-1,utf-8
```

or I can force all files to have *no* coding magic comment with

```ini
[flake8]
no-accept-encodings = True
```

What if I want to accept the implicit encoding too?
I did not expect a C101 when I have this config:

```ini
[flake8]
accept-encodings = utf-8,ascii
```

Because they are the default implicit encodings that resp. Python 3 and 2.7 use when no magic comment is present.

Is there a reason why I shouldn't want this behaviour?

issue_title: Can't set reply-to neither by header nor explicitly
issue_body:
By running README example:

```
mail = EmailMultiAlternatives(
  subject="Your Subject",
  body="This is a simple text email body.",
  from_email="noreply@mydomain.com",
  to=["my@email.com"],
  headers={"Reply-To": "support@sendgrid.com"}
)
mail.send()
```

I get a 400 HTTP error

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/mail/message.py", line 342, in send
    return self.get_connection(fail_silently).send_messages([self])
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sgbackend/mail.py", line 68, in send_messages
    self.sg.client.mail.send.post(request_body=mail)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/python_http_client/client.py", line 204, in http_request
    return Response(self._make_request(opener, request))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/python_http_client/client.py", line 138, in _make_request
    return opener.open(request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/urllib/request.py", line 472, in open
    response = meth(req, response)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/urllib/request.py", line 582, in http_response
    'http', request, response, code, msg, hdrs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/urllib/request.py", line 510, in error
    return self._call_chain(*args)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/urllib/request.py", line 444, in _call_chain
    result = func(*args)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/urllib/request.py", line 590, in http_error_default
    raise HTTPError(req.full_url, code, msg, hdrs, fp)
urllib.error.HTTPError: HTTP Error 400: Bad Request
```

The things is sendgrid-django has a set_reply_to method in the class Mail (https://github.com/sendgrid/sendgrid-python/blob/master/sendgrid/helpers/mail/mail.py#L154) while in sendgrid-django _build_sg_mail method (https://github.com/elbuo8/sendgrid-django/blob/master/sgbackend/mail.py#L69) reply_to is not added by the proper method, instead it expects to be added from setting a custom header like the example does:

```
for k, v in email.extra_headers.items():
    mail.add_header({k: v})
```

Next problem is django EmailMessage class only accepts reply_to param a list/tuple (https://github.com/django/django/blob/1c466994d9729a8902d34e277a3a685bef2a08e1/django/core/mail/message.py#L232) while sengrid-python expects only as I see one single email as reply-to (https://github.com/sendgrid/sendgrid-python/blob/master/sendgrid/helpers/mail/mail.py#L85 and https://github.com/sendgrid/sendgrid-python/blob/ca96c8dcd66224e13b38ab8fd2d2b429dd07dd02/examples/mail/mail.py#L132)

Imagine a situation with this class:
```python
class BigClass:
    def __init__(self, x, y, z, t, w, parent):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y
        self.z = z
        self.t = t
        self.w = w
        self.tmp = TempStorage()
        self.tmp.link(parent)
    ...
    def __repr__(self):
    # other boilerplate
```
It should be possible to refactor this into:
```python
@auto
class BigClass:
    x: int
    y: int
    z: int
    t: int
    w: int
    def initialize(self, parent):  # We can choose other name, maybe name of the class?
        self.tmp = TempStorage()
        self.tmp.link(parent)
```
A simple way to achieve this is to add code like this at the end of generated ``__init__``:
```python
def __init__(self, <inserted args>, *extra_args):
    ...
    if hassattr(self, 'initialize'):
        self.initialize(*extra_args)
```
The pypi source archive isn't including the LICENSE file.  Would it be possible to add it?  It is very helpful when packaging this for Linux distributions.  Thank you.

issue_title: Add back "broken" symbolic links if these were already broken
issue_body: Currently we try to fix the issue when there is a symbolink link pointing to a target that was removed - we remove the link too. This can be an issue if someone intentionally creates a broken symbolic link to be populated later for example by using volumes.

issue_title: Some items return multiple values unexpectedly
issue_body:
The search API (both advancedsearch and scrape) seem to be able to return multiple values in some cases, even where the field is not expected to be able to contain multiple values (like identifier, for example).

At least for the `--itemlist` mode, such multiple-valued items should probably be ignored, with a log message warning about the problem.

Aside from pep8 screaming of redefining of the same function name all the time, wouldn't there be upside to naming those functions for debugging purposes? Now when you'd print COMMANDS_TO_HANDLERS dict, you'd only get {'shortcut': <function _ 0x...>} and there is no telling them apart. While now this is OK, you can find the function by the @cmd decorator, when there is a ton of shortcuts, it'd be much better to have those functions with name.

If you agree, I'll send pull request with renaming.

Summary: Currently some JSON field related database functions accept output_field as parameters, but `JSONExtract` always assumes that `JSONField` is needed, even though the value extracted is a flat type value.

Consider this example:
assume we have a model `Document` with `JSONField` `dynamic_data`. Assume the following content of a `Document` instance's `dynamic_data`:
`{
    'title': 'amazing title'
}`

Currently this query is not possible:
`Document.objects.annotate(title=JSONExtract("dynamic_data", "$.title")).filter(title__icontains="amazing")`
because
`NotImplementedError: Lookup 'icontains' doesn't work with JSONField`
since `JSONField` is hardcoded in `JSONExtract`.

I suggest adding `output_field` parameter to `JSONExtract` so that it would be possible to use `icontains` on annotated data.
MySQL has `FIND_IN_SET`. This could be used to implement model fields that serialize to a set, as a poor man's parallel of the postgres array fields that `django.contrib.postgres` has.

- Implement the [Publisher Acknowledgements](http://www.rabbitmq.com/confirms.html) to await publishing ack's
- Implement [Channel.Flow](https://www.rabbitmq.com/amqp-0-9-1-reference.html#channel.flow) to stop publishing when server asked too.
-  To not send more, than a socket connection can handle to respect [FlowControl](https://www.rabbitmq.com/flow-control.html), or just when network is slow.

issue_title: Work with strings instead of files.
issue_body:
This library looks really handy :+1: 

Having to interact with files is a bit painful for my current use case, though, because I need to change the /etc/hosts on a remote host.
I believe that would mean:

1. `cat /etc/hosts` on the remote host.
2. Write stdout to a local tempfile.
3. Give the path of the tempfile to `Hosts`.
4. Do my changes.
5. `Hosts.write`
6. Read the tempfile.
7. Tell the host to overwrite its /etc/hosts with `str_from_reading_tempfile`.
8. Delete the tempfile.

Is this the correct / most optimal flow?

***

I think things would be simpler if this supported strings directly:

1. `cat /etc/hosts` on the remote host.
2. Feed stdout to `Hosts`.
3. Do my changes.
4. `new_etc_hosts = Hosts.dumps()` (like the `json` module)
5. Tell the host to overwrite its /etc/hosts with `new_etc_hosts`.

Just a thought...

There is an (undocumented, AFAICT) property on the loggers called `parent`. This points to the parent logger, skipping missing loggers up the chain.

Celery uses this property in an interesting way. The `get_task_logger` function overrides that `parent` attribute, so that those loggers will inherit its formatters (which add some nice, useful context about the worker the log was written from, what task was running, etc). Unfortunately, it meant that I saw some very strange behavior from this tool, which assumes that the parent logger will always be something from the beginning of the current logger.

I have tried changing the ODBC driver to ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server, SQL Server Native Client 10, SQL Server Driver (ODBC SQL Server Driver) and had no change in results. My datasource is 64-bit.

issue_title: Files are checked as they are in the workspace rather than the index
issue_body:
Example:
1. Edit foo.py in a way that introduces style errors
2. Run "git add foo.py"
3. Fix style errors in foo.py
4. Run "git commit"

The commit succeeds and commits foo.py with style errors because the linter ran on the workspace version of the file rather than the index.

Imagine a lookup `model_a__model_b__field_c` added to a `ModelAdmin.list_filters`, which employs `AutoOneToOneField` as a relationship field between `model_a` and `model_b`.

Method `lookup_allowed` would return false once you try to use it. This is where it goes wrong: https://github.com/django/django/blame/4420761ea9457d386b2000cf9df5b2f6f88f8f91/django/contrib/admin/options.py#L382

```
            # It is allowed to filter on values that would be found from local
            # model anyways. For example, if you filter on employee__department__id,
            # then the id value would be found already from employee__department_id.
            if not prev_field or (prev_field.is_relation and
                                  field not in prev_field.get_path_info()[-1].target_fields):
                relation_parts.append(part)
```

While debugging I can see that contents of `.target_fields` is `(<annoying.fields.AutoOneToOneField: model_b>,)` at the moment of second iteration, that's what results in `False` for this whole condition here.

Thus the field is being erroneously excluded from `relation_parts` list within the method which later leads to `clean_lookup` becoming `model_a__field_c` instead of its correct form.

Even with these comments I don't fully understand the purpose of this code and what's expected from the field in this case. List of `.target_fields` should be empty? Is that universally correct or just within this code?

Could it be that the bug is within `Django` and not `Annoying` code? Any ideas how to solve it?

P.S. This might be very confusing. Let me know if any clarification is needed.

issue_title: Running a socket server in backgroud?
issue_body:
So I have a socket server that is constantly receiving data from a source. This data comes in bursts and is processed in threads using threading.Thread class. 
```
def fetch(sock):
    while True:
        """
        Runs the process that will continuously fetch data and keep 
        creating new threads to process it.

        sock : A socket.socket object from standard socket library in python
        """
        c_data, address = sock.recvfrom(8129)
        
        # creates a myThread object that is a derived class from threading.Thread
        newthread = myThread(c_data)
        # Runs the thread
        newthread.start()
```
`newthread.start()` runs a function that processes `c_data`  and emits events to connected clients. 

I have to run a eventlet server and have it be capable of handling all the request that a socketIO-client may make and simultaneously keep running this fetch function so that it can keep emitting data to clients. 

The problem:
If I first start the eventlet server the execution of code stops at that line and nothing else can be executed. However if I first start the `fetch()` then execution never reaches the end. 
I am not able to do this using background threads as well. On using background threads it seems that the server starts but it doesn't handle any events emitted from the client.

Following version 2.1.0, the schema view permission class used follows the default settings. This can lead to an unusable spec if authentication is required, and if session auth is not used, leading to a frustrating experience for users wanting to get up and running quickly.

Revert back to a Swagger view rather than wrap the DRF get_schema_view.

Note: endpoint specific permissions should be enforced by providing the `request` to the schema generator.

Relates to #589

I've just taken a stab at migrating from 0.3 to 2.0, and the experience has not been very smooth; to reproduce the old swagger-ui behaviour required a bunch of step-debugging through the new CoreAPI code to determine what was going on.

Worth adding some disclaimers to the migration docs?

* CoreAPI doesn't provide response schemas, which django-rest-swagger 0.3 did. This makes the generated docs less useful. (https://github.com/marcgibbons/django-rest-swagger/issues/583)
* CoreAPI by default doesn't render endpoints which the logged in user doesn't have permissions for. To override this and get the original 0.3 behaviour of displaying all endpoints, you need to subclass the SchemaGenerator thusly:

```
from rest_framework.schemas import SchemaGenerator

class PublicSchemaGenerator(SchemaGenerator):
    def has_view_permissions(self, path, method, view):
        """
        Return `True` if the incoming request has the correct view permissions.
        """
        return True
```

issue_title: dictify method drops custom columns added in includes param
issue_body:
This example in from docs:

```py
import deform
import colander
from colanderalchemy import SQLAlchemySchemaNode
from my.project import SomeClass

typ = colander.String()
widget = deform.widget.SelectWidget(values=(('foo', 'a'),
                                            ('bar', 'b'),
                                            ('baz', 'c')))
column = colander.SchemaNode(typ,
                             name='customfield',
                             widget=widget)
schema = SQLAlchemySchemaNode(SomeClass,
                              includes=['name', column, 'biography'],
                              excludes=['id'],
                              title='Some class')
```

Illustrates that you can add custom columns that do not exist on the provided SQLAlchemy table to the colander schema by passing it in the includes list.  However the `dictify` method uses an inspector object based on that SQLA table to verify that the attribute exists, thus any columns added only in the includes will be dropped by the method.  See the [key portion](https://github.com/stefanofontanelli/ColanderAlchemy/blob/master/colanderalchemy/schema.py#L595) of the code here:

```py
try:
    getattr(self.inspector.column_attrs, name)
    value = getattr(obj, name)

except AttributeError:
    try:
        prop = getattr(self.inspector.relationships, name)
        if prop.uselist:
            value = [self[name].children[0].dictify(o)
                     for o in getattr(obj, name)]
        else:
            o = getattr(obj, name)
            value = None if o is None else self[name].dictify(o)
```

issue_title: This implementation can not handle nested/multilevel dictionaries properly
issue_body:
I caught this a month ago in my fork as the issue for many bugs during integration testing -- the current implementation does not catch nested dict values changing.

eg the following code will not automatically persist:

* Request 1: `sesssion.data = {'foo': {'bar': 1}}`
* Request 2: `sesssion.data['foo']['bar'] = 2`

Persisting Request 2 to redis requires an explicit call to `session.changed`

I handled it in my fork by calculating a md5 hash of the from_redis string on from_redis (https://github.com/jvanasco/pyramid_redis_sessions/blob/custom_deployment/pyramid_redis_sessions/session.py#L189-L198).   If an explicit persist is not requested in my  finished callback (https://github.com/jvanasco/pyramid_redis_sessions/blob/custom_deployment/pyramid_redis_sessions/__init__.py#L354-L371), I check to see if the current hashed value is different via a `should_persist` query (https://github.com/jvanasco/pyramid_redis_sessions/blob/custom_deployment/pyramid_redis_sessions/session.py#L52-L61)

there are probably better ways to detect change, but calculating an md5 value is negligible and quick to implement.  

i backported the test case for your version (attached)

```
 TestRedisSession(unittest.TestCase):
    ...
    def test_dict_multilevel(self):
        inst = self._set_up_session_in_Redis_and_makeOne(session_id='test1')
        inst['dict'] = {'foo': {'bar': 1}}
        get_from_inst = inst['dict']['foo']['bar']
        self.assertEqual(get_from_inst, 1)
        session_dict_in_redis = inst.from_redis()['managed_dict']
        get_from_redis = session_dict_in_redis['dict']['foo']['bar']
        self.assertEqual(get_from_redis, 1)
        inst['dict']['foo']['bar'] = 2
        ## requires a call to `changed()` under current system
        # inst.changed()
        session_dict_in_redis2 = inst.from_redis()['managed_dict']
        get_from_redis2 = session_dict_in_redis2['dict']['foo']['bar']
        self.assertEqual(get_from_redis2, 2)
```

issue_body:
Probably the same problem than #224. I have this model:

```python
class Model(Document):
    field = fields.MapField(fields.EmbeddedDocumentField('OtherModel'), help_text='help')
```

On the API, the help text does **not** appear whereas it does with a `StringField` for instance.

The help text **does** appear when I add it into `extra_kwargs`:

```python
class Serializer(DocumentSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Model
        fields = '__all__'
        extra_kwargs = {'field': {'help_text': 'help'}}
```

Thanks!

issue_title: Serializer parameter `depth` should not restrict embedded documents
issue_body:
Rationale:
1. The restriction is to avoid recursive circular references, embedded documents can not have such recursion and the restriction makes no sense
2. At bottom of depth, the reference field is easily serialized as an id-field. For embedded doc there is no meaningful replacement, it comes just as loss of data.

issue_title: Add options to turn on preflight check for file uploads
issue_body: Right now for normal file upload interfaces, we don't provide an option to turn on preflight before actually upload the file to Box. We can add that as a keyword argument default to False.

There is a (recently introduced) inconsistency between Row.get(key) and Row[key]

Where the Row is the result of a join, you can access fields in the sub-Rows like Row["tablename.fieldname"] - but not like Row.get("tablename.fieldname").

In earlier versions, the behavior of Row[key] and Row.get(key) was the same - changing the behavior of Row.get(key) breaks large parts of our code (not backwards-compatible), and it also seems very inconsistent and counter-intuitive compared with both normal Python dicts as well as gluon.storage.Storage.

issue_title: How to configure a GBDT model by mxnet？
issue_body: it is said that mxnet supports the GBDT  algorithm， how can I invoking it

## Description

It is currently not possible to run distributed training with `kvstore='dist_async'`, when using custom operators. (details below)

## Environment info (Required)
Python 3 + MXNet compiled with `USE_DIST_KVSTORE =1`

## Build info (Required if built from source)

MXNet compiled with `USE_DIST_KVSTORE =1`

## Steps to reproduce

Use `kvstore='dist_async'` with a network that contains a custom operator registered using `@mx.operator.register("my_custom_op")`.

## Detailed problem description

When using `kvstore='dist_async'` the parameter updates are run on the parameter server. In order to do so the optimizer is pickled and sent to the sever. Among other things the optimizer holds a reference to the symbol (see [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/optimizer.py#L104)). Now when the server loads the pickled optimizer object it tries to instantiate the symbol. This works fine only if the symbol does not use any custom object. The reason for this is the following: In order to tell mxnet about custom operators one needs to use the `@mx.operator.register`  annotation. So for the server one would need to first register the class by importing the module containing it and only then start the server. The problem is that the server gets started as soon as mxnet is important, so that one does not have any chance of ever registering the custom operator on the server, see [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/kvstore_server.py#L85).

Ideally one would write the server code something like this:
```
# register op with mxnet
from module_with_custom_op import custom_op
import mxnet.kvstore_server

mxnet.kvstore_server._init_kvstore_server_module()
```
where `mxnet.kvstore_server` no longer automatically starts the server.

When I tried to create a ccsgd optimizer like this:

```
optimizer = mx.optimizer.ccSGD()
```

From the source code:

```

            [momentum, kwargs['rescale_grad'], kwargs['clip_gradient']])
```

It seems if I dont pass rescale_grad and clip_gradient explicitly, it will pop up this error. Since they all have default values, can I just use the default ones?

issue_title: Any special reason for the default initializer mx.init.Xavier(factor_type="in", magnitude=2.34) to take the magnitude 2.34?
issue_body:
The Xavier initializer is mx.init.Xavier(factor_type="in", magnitude=3), and
The MSRA initializer  is mx.init.Xavier(factor_type="in", rnd_type="gaussian", magnitude=2).

Why the default initializer adopts a strange magnitude of 2.34, any special reason for this?

## Description
Dear all, am (completely) new to mxnet. I am trying to implement  a simple [unet](https://arxiv.org/abs/1505.04597)  architecture  for semantic segmentation with mxnet. 
[](url)
This is my unet implementation: 
```Python
class UNet(Block):
    def __init__(self,**kwargs):
        super(UNet, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        
        self.Nfilters = 32
        self.Nbatch = 1 # to be addressed later 
        self.NClasses = 11
        self.img_size = 192
        self.axis = 1 # For NCHW format 
        
        with self.name_scope():
            # 32
            self.conv11=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch11=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv12=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch12=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.pool1 = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2,strides=2)
            
            # 64
            self.conv21=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch21=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv22=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch22=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.pool2 = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2,strides=2)
            
            # 128 
            self.conv31=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**2,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch31=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv32=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**2,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch32=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.pool3 = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2,strides=2)
            
            # 256
            self.conv41=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**3,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch41=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv42=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**3,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch42=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.pool4 = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=self.axis,strides=2)
            
            # 512
            self.conv51=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**4,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch51=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv52=gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**4,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch52=gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            #self.pool5 = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2,strides=1,layout='NHWC')

            
            self.up6 = gluon.nn.Conv2DTranspose(self.Nfilters*2**3,kernel_size=(2,2), strides=(2,2)) # Will go in to the 
            
            self.conv61 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**3,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch61= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv62 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**3,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch62= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            
            
            self.up7 = gluon.nn.Conv2DTranspose(self.Nfilters*2**2,kernel_size=(2,2), strides=(2,2))
            
            self.conv71 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**2,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch71= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv72 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**2,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch72= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            
             
            self.up8 = gluon.nn.Conv2DTranspose(self.Nfilters*2**1,kernel_size=(2,2), strides=(2,2))
            
            self.conv81 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**1,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch81= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv82 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**1,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch82= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            

             
            self.up9 = gluon.nn.Conv2DTranspose(self.Nfilters*2**1,kernel_size=(2,2), strides=(2,2))
            
            self.conv91 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**1,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch91= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv92 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**1,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch92= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)


            self.up10 = gluon.nn.Conv2DTranspose(self.Nfilters*2**0,kernel_size=(2,2), strides=(2,2))
            
            self.conv101 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**0,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch101= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)
            self.conv102 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.Nfilters*2**0,kernel_size=3,padding=1,activation='relu')
            self.batch102= gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=self.axis, center=True, scale=True)

            
            self.convLast = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.NClasses,kernel_size=(1,1),padding=0)
            
            
            
    def forward(self,x):
        
        conv1 = self.conv11(x)
        conv1 = self.batch11(conv1)
        conv1 = self.conv12(conv1)
        conv1 = self.batch12(conv1)
        pool1 = self.pool1(conv1)
            
            
        conv2 = self.conv21(pool1)
        conv2 = self.batch21(conv2)
        conv2 = self.conv22(conv2)
        conv2 = self.batch22(conv2)
        pool2 = self.pool2(conv2)
        
        conv3 = self.conv31(pool2)
        conv3 = self.batch31(conv3)
        conv3 = self.conv32(conv3)
        conv3 = self.batch32(conv3)
        pool3 = self.pool3(conv3)
            
            
        conv4 = self.conv41(pool3)
        conv4 = self.batch41(conv4)
        conv4 = self.conv42(conv4)
        conv4 = self.batch42(conv4)
        pool4 = self.pool4(conv4)

        
        conv5 = self.conv51(pool4)
        conv5 = self.batch51(conv5)
        conv5 = self.conv52(conv5)
        conv5 = self.batch52(conv5)
        
        # UpSampling with transposed Convolution
        conv6 = self.up6(conv5)
        conv6 = mx.nd.concat(conv6,conv4)
        conv6 = self.conv61(conv6)
        conv6 = self.batch61(conv6)
        conv6 = self.conv62(conv6)
        conv6 = self.batch62(conv6)

        # UpSampling with transposed Convolution
        conv7 = self.up7(conv6)
        conv7 = mx.nd.concat(conv7,conv3)
        conv7 = self.conv71(conv7)
        conv7 = self.batch71(conv7)
        conv7 = self.conv72(conv7)
        conv7 = self.batch72(conv7)

        # UpSampling with transposed Convolution
        conv8 = self.up8(conv7)
        conv8 = mx.nd.concat(conv8,conv2)
        conv8 = self.conv81(conv8)
        conv8 = self.batch81(conv8)
        conv8 = self.conv82(conv8)
        conv8 = self.batch82(conv8)


        # UpSampling with transposed Convolution
        conv9 = self.up9(conv8)
        conv9 = mx.nd.concat(conv9,conv1)
        conv9 = self.conv91(conv9)
        conv9 = self.batch91(conv9)
        conv9 = self.conv92(conv9)
        conv9 = self.batch92(conv9)

        final_layer = self.convLast(conv9)
        
        # Experimental 
        final_layer = nd.reshape(final_layer,(self.Nbatch,self.NClasses*self.img_size*self.img_size))
        final_layer = nd.SoftmaxActivation(final_layer)
        final_layer = nd.reshape(final_layer,(self.Nbatch,self.NClasses,self.img_size,self.img_size))
        
        return final_layer

```

issue_body: I see that there are some dependencies like cython. Please provide a requirements.txt and also update the values in setup.py so that they also get installed while doing the pip install

This issue is to track adding TCP based listener(s) to the watchman service.
Our model has been to use unix domain sockets where available so that we can dodge some authentication issues.

On Windows, we have a mostly-working layer that uses named pipes, but the model for these is rather different from unix domain sockets, due to the limiting APIs available in various clients.  Debugging on Windows is difficult due to lack of nice tools.

The high level idea to make things more pleasant and easier to work with by removing the named pipe stuff and replacing it with TCP sockets on Windows.  This has the nice side effect that we can also enable the same code on Unix systems and have it tested as part of our integration test suite, and it makes it possible to use comfortable system tracing and profiling tools to figure out problems that arise.

The idea is that we can come up with a simple scheme that looks enough like unix domain sockets that there won't be a massive change to the internals.  Here's the game plan:

* The server creates a regular file in place of the unix domain socket in the filesystem
* We'll refer to this file as the `sockfile` below
* The contents of the `sockfile` describe how to connect to the server over TCP
* Clients can stat the sockpath to determine whether it is a file or a socket and react accordingly (but can short cut that step on Windows where it can only be a file and not a socket)

I've really wanted to avoid any serious authentication code because it is notoriously tricky to get it right and to deal with various authentication schemes.  At this time we still only want to enable local connections to the server, but it is conceivable that we may want to enable some advanced remote/relay use cases with watchman in the future.  With that in mind, we'd like to be able to support more than a very basic authentication scheme.

To allow forwards compatibility, the file that was described above will be a JSON object with the following fields:

* `credentials` - describes the authentication credentials to be used.
* `listeners` - is an array listing the available endpoints

An example might look like this:

```json
{
   "credentials": {
      "username": "myname",
      "password": "secret"
   },
   "listeners": [
      "tcp:[::1]:1234",
      "tcp:127.0.0.1:1234"
   ]
}
```

The above shows how we might indicate that the watchman service is running on port 1234 on both IPv4 and IPv6 loopback addresses, and that the client should use the specified credentials when it connects.

## Accommodating authentication in the watchman protocol

When we start up a listener, we can annotate the default state as being implicit (`EXTERNAL` in the SASL terminology) or whether authentication is needed.  When authentication is needed, the client session will only allow a very limited selection of commands to be used; just those related to setting up an authenticated session.  Once authentication is complete, the whole command set is made available.

We should be able to support SASL rather than narrowly focusing on our immediate need.  That means that the authentication process would go something like:

* Client sends a version command requesting authentication capabilities
* If the server supports authentication, the version command will include a list of configured SASL mechanisms
* If the client is configured to use authentication, it selects the most appropriate mechanism and performs authentication via the new `authenticate` command

## The authenticate command

SASL allows for challenge-response authentication, where the client informs the server about the mechanism that it intends to use, and the server sends some kind of challenge string to initiate the credential exchange, then the client responds with the credential data.  For a simple password scheme, this is a bit chatty, so a more terse form can be used where the client just sends the credentials along with the mechanism.  The two examples are shown below:

```
C:> ["authenticate", {"mech": "PLAIN"}]
S:< {"authenticate": false, "challenge":""}
C:> ["authenticate",{"username":"myname", "password":"secret"}]
S:<{"authenticate": true, "identity":"myname"}
```

This is the preferred, less chatty, version:

```
C:> ["authenticate", {"mech": "PLAIN","username":"myname", "password":"secret"}]
S:<{"authenticate": true, "identity":"myname"}
```

I only really want to support this `PLAIN` use case for now, and to do so without *requiring* the use of something relatively heavyweight, such as libsasl, because I'd like to keep the number of dependencies low enough that we stand a chance of being able to build watchman on Windows without much fuss.

## Configuring credentials

I'd like to avoid maintaining the credentials persistently, so the initial thought is that we'd have the default (and only) mode of operation be that the watchman service would compute a randomized set of credentials when it is initialized and write those to the credentials section of the `sockfile`.  The `sockfile` would be readable only to the owner of the watchman process, which gives it similar properties to unix domain socket access control; the filesystem enforces access.

## What do we need to change and where?

* Plan to remove `stream_win.c`
* Add `stream_tcp.c` or adapt `stream_unix.c` to support TCP
* Add the `authenticate` command
* Add an authenticated status field in the server side watchman_client struct and reject unauthenticated commands
* Modify `listener.c` to bring up the TCP listener based on a global flag that defaults to true on Windows
* Initial TCP listener implementation will randomly assign a port (bind to port `0` and let the kernel assign it) and compute the credentials and generate the `sockfile`.  Future iterations can make this more configurable, but that is not necessary to support Windows.
* pywatchman and fb-watchman (nodejs client) will be updated to test whether the sockfile is a socket or file and adapt accordingly
* pywatchman changes will then need to be synced in to the hgwatchman repo (which may well be in the main mercurial repo by the time we're ready for this)

plone 4.3.4 | plone 4.3.5

issue_title: socket.recv() does't throw exception when interrupted by a signal
issue_body:
Hi,

socket.recv doesn't throw exception when interrupted by a signal since pyzmq version 14.7.0. This makes it impossible or hard to cleanly exit a program by using signal (like SIGINT).

The following script illustrates the bug:
```python
import signal
import zmq

print("Current libzmq version is %s" % zmq.zmq_version())
print("Current pyzmq version is %s" % zmq.__version__)

def signal_handler(signum, frame):
    print("Signal handler called")
    pass

ctx = zmq.Context()
socket = ctx.socket(zmq.REP)
socket.bind("tcp://*:5555")

signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal_handler)
try:
    socket.recv()
except zmq.ZMQError as e:
    print(e)
```

Example run on pyzmq 14.6.0:
```
Current libzmq version is 4.0.4
Current pyzmq version is 14.6.0
^CSignal handler called
Interrupted system call
```

Example (failed) run on pyzmq 14.7.0:
```
Current libzmq version is 4.0.4
Current pyzmq version is 14.7.0
^CSignal handler called
^CSignal handler called
^CSignal handler called
^CSignal handler called
^\Quit
```

issue_title: keyservice exits without notice when socket not available
issue_body:
If some other process still is attached to the specified unix socket, the key service will exit without any such indication:
```
$ strace sops keyservice --net unix --addr $HOME/.sops/keyservice.sock --verbose
execve("/usr/local/bin/sops", ["sops", "keyservice", "--net", "unix", "--addr", "/home/hwine/.sops/keyservice.soc"..., "--verbose"], [/* 30 v
ars */]) = 0
brk(NULL)                               = 0x135d000
...
clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, {20468, 379425000}) = 0
socket(PF_LOCAL, SOCK_STREAM|SOCK_CLOEXEC|SOCK_NONBLOCK, 0) = 3
setsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_BROADCAST, [1], 4) = 0
setsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, [1], 4) = 0
bind(3, {sa_family=AF_LOCAL, sun_path="/home/hwine/.sops/keyservice.sock"}, 36) = -1 EADDRINUSE (Address already in use)
close(3)                                = 0
exit_group(0)                           = ?
+++ exited with 0 +++
```

At least on WSL, the mere existence of the socket file is enough to report "address in use", even if no keyservice is running.

Python 2.6 is no longer supported by the Python core team, please upgrade your Python.

This is the DeprecationWarning coming while running tests on travis with Python 2.6

A user types `this & that` into a text field, which I then bleach :

`title = bleach.clean(title, strip=True, tags=[])`

bleach converts the ampersand into an html entity (`&`)

bleach provides means to whitelist tags, attributes and styles, but no way to whitelist special characters. Now my only option is to mark the field "safe" on output.... but I don't really want to do that. 

Is there no way to prevent bleach from converting ampersands into entities?
