issue_title: Proposal: update etcdctl on all new CoreOS hosts
issue_body:
Currently, we update etcdctl on CoreOS hosts _if_ one has opted into the graceful shutdown unit.

What if we just update etcdctl on _all_ new CoreOS clusters instead?  This would significantly aid troubleshooting efforts since newer versions expose things like `member list` and `cluster-health`.

This shouldn't break anything.  Any containerized component would continue to use the older etcdctl 0.4.6 to ensure backwards compatibility with older clusters.

Code:

            pad_left,
            pad_right,
        )**

Error message:
**"Exception Value: | cannot import name 'pad_left'"**

### How can it be fixed?

It looks like in the more recent version of eth_utils, there is no longer a file called "formatting.py" which contains the function defs for pad_left() and pad_right() functions.  There is also no longer an import for these in the __init__.py in the root "eth_utils" folder.

issue_title: GIT_DESCRIBE becomes stale for local build
issue_body: For local build, where the GIT_URL is something like "./"  the git_cache becomes up to date and the git build string stays old

issue_title: MatchSpec should differentiate between package version requested or not
issue_body: It appears that MatchSpec treats a package requested as the same as pinning it to the latest version as well. However it would be ideal if MatchSpec treated a package requested without a version as a mere requirement of having the package and not the package on its latest version. This would allow for graceful downgrading of the package should new installations require it.

issue_title: Conda env removes default kernelspec without replacing it
issue_body: Notebooks with the kernel "`python3`" cannot be opened with the conda env kernelspec manager (`nb_conda_kernels` version `1.0.3`). This seems to be because nb_conda_kernels removes the default kernelspec but does not put anything to replace it. A solution would be for the kernelspec corresponding to the Root env to reuse this key rather than following the naming scheme for other envs. Alternately, it could not remove the default kernelspec. The result looks like the following image

issue_title: New networking filter causing problems with anaconda
issue_body:
The network security team recently set up a https filter thus causing a ton of problems with trying to get anaconda to run the way it's supposed to be. I got them to whitelist some sites which allows me to log into anaconda navigator and install/update packages, but i am still having issues with anaconda navigator opening up as it gets stuck at the 'loading applications' step for 10 minutes at a time. I am also having issues with creating new environments in navigator where the entire program goes into 'not responding' for even longer than 10 minutes. It eventually builds the environment but it's a total hassle every time i open the app and make environments. 

What are the https addresses i need to have network security whitelist in the filter in order to get full functionality back into anaconda navigator?

issue_title: Why need PrefixReplaceLinkAction? It breaks Windows permissions.
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior If the conda environment is read-only to Users, installing as admin packages with files that have "prefix_placeholder" overwrite these files permissions to have no access to Users and makes these packages unusable. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. There is a base installation of Anaconda in C:\Anaconda with permissions inherited from C:\: * Administrators read/write * Users read-only 2. However, C:\Anaconda\Library\Qt5Core.dll permissions are different and explicit: * Administrators read/write * Administrator user read/write * Users - no access 3. `conda uninstall qt` and `conda install qt` as administrator user do not change anything 4. Upon further investigation it turns out that in C:\Anaconda\pkgs\qt-5.9.6-vc14h62aca36_0\info\paths.json the entry for Qt5Core.dll has prefix placeholder: ``` { "_path": "Library/bin/Qt5Core.dll", "file_mode": "binary", "path_type": "hardlink", "prefix_placeholder": "C:/qt5/_h_env", "sha256": "50886da211b4acaacc015e18f52fe44c2a07500f05fd09a9cb3a5c026a5eaba4", "size_in_bytes": 5241344 }, ``` 5. The TRACE output of `conda install qt` shows that in this case PrefixReplaceLinkAction is used: ``` TRACE conda.core.path_actions:verify(385): copying C:\Anaconda\pkgs\qt-5.9.6-vc14h62aca36_0\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll => C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 TRACE conda.gateways.disk.create:_do_copy(275): copying C:\Anaconda\pkgs\qt-5.9.6-vc14h62aca36_0\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll => C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 TRACE conda.core.path_actions:verify(390): rewriting prefixes in C:\Anaconda\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll ``` 6. The temp directory is created by mkdtemp() and the [documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html#tempfile.mkdtemp) says that > The directory is readable, writable, and searchable only by the creating user ID. 7. This operation first copies the file from pkgs folder to temporary folder that is only writable by current user and later it hardlinks the file from this directory into C:\Anaconda\Library\bin. ``` TRACE conda.core.path_actions:execute(415): linking C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 => C:\Anaconda\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll TRACE conda.gateways.disk.create:create_link(321): hard linking C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 => C:\Anaconda\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll ``` 8. The hardlinks retain source file's permissions, so now, the DLL is not readable by other Users. ## Expected Behavior I do not understand why the treatment for files with prefix_placeholder is so radically different. Why conda cannot hardlink the file from pkgs folder as other files? Why copy into temp folder with specifically minimal permissions? The workaround could be to not use system temp folder, but create a temp folder in conda pkgs directory so that permissions are retained. ## Environment Information
`conda info`

issue_title: conda 4.4.0rc1 not functioning with fish shell
issue_body:
I've done a lot of digging into this and can't seem to figure it out. I see that conda previously had fish support (e.g. https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/545, #2611, #2680, etc.) but I'm not having any luck getting it to work on 4.40rc1.

`> source ~/miniconda3/etc/fish/conf.d/conda.fish` completes silently, without error.

Subsequently, running 'conda' from a fish shell results in the following:

```
> conda
fish: Unknown command 'conda'
conda.fish (line 2): 
        command conda
                ^
in function 'conda'
	called on standard input
```

I tried to install Cuda and Caffe (via Homebrew), and for that purpose I did many changes system-wide, like uninstalling Homebrew python and downgrading command line tools. (these changes have now been reverted)

Now anaconda is behaving oddly. **`conda <flag>` returns nothing**.

**I can `source activate envs`, but I get a blank prompt for any `conda install`, `conda info`, `conda debug`**, the lot.

I have since then reinstalled `anaconda2`, checked my path on `.profile`, and it's all there.

```
echo $PATH:

/Users/me/anaconda2/bin:/Users/me/anaconda2/bin:/Users/me/anaconda2/bin:/usr/bin:~/bin:/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/Users/me/anaconda2/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:/opt/X11/bin:/usr/local/MacGPG2/bin
```
also, I ran `brew doctor`, and it tells me this:

```
Warning: "config" scripts exist outside your system or Homebrew directories.
`./configure` scripts often look for *-config scripts to determine if
software packages are installed, and what additional flags to use when
compiling and linking.

Having additional scripts in your path can confuse software installed via
Homebrew if the config script overrides a system or Homebrew provided
script of the same name. We found the following "config" scripts:
```

issue_title: Add "Portable" command line option to miniconda installer
issue_body:
We've tried to deploy a web application on Azure's web apps, using the silent install functionality of the miniconda installer. Since we're deploying to a sandbox there, we can't modify global machine state, such as the registry. We thought that we could install portably, but of course, among others, the installer registers the uninstaller, and this causes the deployment to fail. See https://github.com/projectkudu/kudu/issues/1862

I've tried to find the source for your .msi installer so I could open a PR, but I guess they're closed source as I couldn't find them. Can you add an option to the installer so miniconda installs 100% portable, e.g. without touching the registry, program files and environment variables? Alternatively, please open-source the installer so I can submit a PR?

issue_title: Make variant page gene panels info link to corresponding scout panel view
Need a way to import from hdf5 into existing ctable
issue_body:
See mailing list discussion [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/bcolz/jLg-WUmb95g) and StackOverflow question [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33179895/convert-multi-node-pytable-to-bcolz).

Basically what is needed is a way to say "load this node from this HDF5 file and put it in this ctable, even if the ctable already exists".  Currently an error is raised if you try to use `fromhdf5` with a `rootdir` that already exists.  But sometimes what you want to do is import multiple HDF5 nodes into a single ctable, without creating intermediate separate tables for each node.

issue_title: extracting rows from tables fails with np.object column
issue_body:
This code will demonstrate the error on master:

```
import bcolz
import numpy as np

dtype = np.dtype([("a", np.object),
                  ("b", np.uint8),
                  ("c", np.int32),
                  ("d", np.float32)
                  ])

a = bcolz.ctable(np.empty(0, dtype=dtype), mode="w")

a.append(("aaaaa", 23, 34567, 1.2355))
a.append(("aaaaa", 23, 34567, 1.2355))
a.append(("aaaaa", 23, 34567, 1.2355))
a.append(("aaaaa", 23, 34567, 1.2355))

print a[np.array([1, 0, 2])]
```

the error is:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "t.py", line 17, in <module>
    print a[np.array([1, 0, 2])]
  File "/install/data/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bcolz/ctable.py", line 1087, in __getitem__
    return np.array([self[i] for i in key], dtype=self.dtype)
ValueError: Setting void-array with object members using buffer.
```

This fix is to change:
```
return np.array([self[i] for i in key], dtype=self.dtype)
```
to:
```
return np.array([tuple(self[i]) for i in key], dtype=self.dtype)
```
at the offending line in bcolz/ctable.py

issue_title: Documentation Issue: when deleting snapshots curator's exit status is non-zero if snapshots don't exist, but docs do not mention this or any other result codes.
issue_body:
At first I thought this was just inconsistent behavior and was going to report it, but I checked the result code and curator returns `99` when no snapshots exist that meet snapshot criteria like `--older-than`.

Task runners and other tools like Cron or Rundeck look for a zero exit status. Programs that use exit status for more than just success or failure should document those statuses so that the tool can be used appropriately.

issue_title: Surrogates from poorly formed Unicode breaks transport layer
issue_body:
In any situation where you are ingesting content into ElasticSearch that you do not directly control, there is the possibility that someone somewhere along the lines has improperly encoded UTF-16 or another encoding to UTF-8 through the use of surrogate pairs.

These are not supported in UTF-8, in Python, or in ElasticSearch, and will cause mountains of errors like this:

` File \"/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/elasticsearch/transport.py\", line 295, in perform_request
   body = body.encode('utf-8')
UnicodeEncodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\udd9e' in position 19281: surrogates not allowed`

The fix is quite simple, surrogate escapes must be backslash replaced in Python3:
`try:
            body.encode("utf-8")
        except UnicodeEncodeError as e:
            if e.reason == 'surrogates not allowed':
                body = body.encode('utf-8', "backslashreplace").decode('utf-8')`

I will open a pull request with this fix.

issue_title: Command Authoring: single options_list alias values require an empty second element with comma
issue_body:
This is an invalid param binding, but doesn't obviously look invalid: 
`register_cli_argument('sample command', 'credential_user_name', options_list=('--user-name'))`

The error you get during load testing is this: 
ValueError: invalid option string 'n': must start with a character '-' 

That error is confusing and doesn't actually help to resolve the problem. The problem is that there must be a comma ',' after '--user-name', which is not intuitive in most cases, since you won't have any elements after the comma here is the valid command:
``register_cli_argument('sample command', 'credential_user_name', options_list=('--user-name',))`

This is very difficult to track down and fix when it fails and very easy to forget to do.

issue_title: Command to return free priority level in range
issue_body:
Hi,
I see it useful to have such api call in az cli:
```
az network nsg rule create \
    **--priority-lowest 1000 \**
    **--priority-highest 2000 \**
    --name <rule-name> \
    --nsg-name <network-security-group> \
    --resource-group <resource-group>
```

As result I expect adding new security rule with priority in range of 1000 and 2000.

At the moment creating new security rule requires specifying priority level. In some situations such accuracy makes sense, but most of the time we just want to add new security rule without putting so much sense into it's priority. I believe it's a common practice that developers come up with custom scripting logic to select any available priority level - which is source of errors and is repeated in every client scripts.

Proposed approach shifts from specifying exact value of priority to providing a possibility to design different priority layers/levels and place new rules in these layers, meaning that all rules in one layer have "same" priority from business perspective.

For example:
- 100..1000 important rules
- 1000..2000 regular rules
- 2000..4096 temporary added rules

issue_title: AADTokenCredentials raises TokenExpiredError at first, but then token is immediately refreshed
issue_body:
When using AADTokenCredentials and the token expires the client ends up raising a TokenExpiredError, but the token looks like it's refreshed.  After that, doing the same calls to the client is successful.  I wrote an example script to try to illustrate it:

```
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

from adal import AuthenticationContext
from msrestazure.azure_active_directory import AADTokenCredentials
from azure.mgmt.subscription import SubscriptionClient


def expire_token(token):
    expiry = (datetime.now() - timedelta(seconds=creds.token['expires_in'])).timestamp()
    token['expires_at'] = token['expires_on'] = expiry
    token['expires_in'] = 0

def create_auth():
    context = AuthenticationContext(authority='https://login.microsoftonline.com/canadagoose.onmicrosoft.com')
    code = context.acquire_user_code(resource='https://management.core.windows.net', client_id='04b07795-8ddb-461a-bbee-02f9e1bf7b46')
    print(code['message'])
    token = context.acquire_token_with_device_code('https://management.core.windows.net', user_code_info=code, client_id='04b07795-8ddb-461a-bbee-02f9e1bf7b46')
    creds = AADTokenCredentials(token=token, client_id='04b07795-8ddb-461a-bbee-02f9e1bf7b46')

    return creds

creds = create_auth()
sub_client = SubscriptionClient(creds)

for sub in sub_client.subscriptions.list():
    print(sub.display_name)

expire_token(creds.token)

# Raises msrest.exceptions.TokenExpiredError, but token is refreshed

for sub in sub_client.subscriptions.list():

print('Token expiry after', datetime.fromtimestamp(creds.token['expires_at']))

issue_title: BlobService class blobs with "question marks" in the path issue

please advice,

Lior

issue_title: computer-readable output when run in a CI job
issue_body:
I'd really like to use pre-commit for running in a CI job, and output a json blob (or other serialized primitive format). I think that pre-commit is great for being used as a runner for a large set of code quality tests.
I'm already looking into doing the legwork on this, but I wanted to get your opinions on this. 

So far, the feature list I'm implementing/"requiring" is:
- --json-output=filepath flag - there's no sane way to head off all of the prints, so this would probably have to dump to a tempfile somewhere. 
- replace the return value of ints with an intermediate retval interface between hooks and pre-commit main - with backcompat, of course. That way, I can encapsulate all text outputs in a list[string] and pass them back to the caller. 
- update existing hooks with an intermediate print() replacement, and optional argument to use the intermediate retval.
- Some interesting pre-commit hooks that I need (and will contribute back, if possible).

I'm posting this before I start the bulk of my work, to make sure I'm not stepping on any toes. At the least, I can fork and maybe bring it back in? Please tear this request up.

Btw, I've already started using pre-commit for our needs in a prototype capacity, and I'm really happy with it.

## Long story short

Returning a response with `force_close()` before the request is fully read still causes lingering.

## Expected behaviour

Connection should be closed immediately after the response is written.

## Actual behaviour

The lingering logic in `web_protocol.py` is activated.

issue_title: Why - 'Concurrent call to receive() is not allowed'
issue_body: Can I find out what is the technical reason for this restriction?

## Long story short

New issue for https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/2098

Proposal is:

 * drop auto-cancellation for next major release 3.x
 * cancellation can be controlled by user by new `request` property, 
for example 
```python
async def handler(request):
    request.cancel_on_lost = False
```
As well on route level
```python
app.router.add_get('/', handle, cancel_on_lost=True)
```
Or even on application level
```python
app = web.Application(cancel_on_lost=True)
```

Default parameter for all ways should be `False`

https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/b348e0f47c8ad16f898fd0dffa00c814b259131e/aiohttp/web_protocol.py#L198 should check `request.cancel_on_lost` attribute

## Expected behaviour

Cancellation should be controlled by end user

## Actual behaviour

There is no way to disable cancellation

## Steps to reproduce

https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/2098

## Long story short

I'm working on a long running program that makes a significant number of https requests, and the memory of the program increases steadily.

## Expected behaviour

I would expect the memory to remain relatively constant.

## Actual behaviour

For a sample 500 requests the memory increases approximate 8 meg doing just requests.

## Steps to reproduce

```py
import logging
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import gc


async def get():
    response = await aiohttp.request('get', 'https://ddunlop.com')
    logging.info(response.status)
    response.close()


async def main(loop):
    await asyncio.sleep(10)
    for x in range(0, 500):
        await get()

    logging.info('done fetching')
    gc.collect()
    await asyncio.sleep(60)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    logging.getLogger().setLevel('DEBUG')
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main(loop))
```

## Your environment

aiohttp 2.0.2 inside a `python:3.6.0-slim` docker container.

The new supported function is `asyncio.ensure_future`. The deprecation causes warnings to appear in our tests.

Unfortunately, `ensure_future` doesn't appear to be supported in the versions of the asyncio library that are available for Python 3.3. We could introduce a hack to choose a different function depending on what Python we're in. Or we could consider dropping support for 3.3. I don't think it's got very widespread usage, and supporting it complicates our setup.py.

I'm leaning towards the hack, since it should be pretty contained. Just wanted to think about it.
We should add more options in skbuild's `setup()` function to customize more aspects of the build.

issue_title: Support for skipping compiling the test project
issue_body:
In the current implementation, a small test project is always compiler using the selected generator, config and CMake option starting with `-DCMAKE_`.

issue_title: add wallet upgrade instructions to readme.md
issue_body:
Noticed that we do not mention wallet instructions for upgrading `blockstack-core` in the README.md.

Missing things like:
`$ blockstack-core setup_wallet`

@shea256 points out that we not only need per-device keychains (one for your laptop, one for your phone, etc.), but also that these keychains must be external to Blockstack.  This is because your phone can't run a Core node, or have any long-running process.

What this means for Core is that there's some more work to be done on the storage end of things:

* A name's zone files should have the signing public key *for each device* in its zone file.  For example, my zone file might have something like this:

```
$ORIGIN judecn.id
$TTL 3600
pubkey.19db6d10-5cac-43d3-a055-b11e9d0e6cc7 TXT "pubkey:data:03cabba0b5b9a871dbaa11c044066e281c5feb57243c7d2a452f06a0d708613a46
pubkey.c6b22bea-379c-424a-925c-9d3aad465128 TXT "pubkey:data:0267cd02e6ceae7ed58e3c8ca856633bf5424ccce73c44f0eb7b71d1fe3a6f5e0f
```

where my device-specific public keys are named as `pubkey.{uuid4}`.

* Devices need a way to share their datastore-specific public keys.  This is problematic, because datastore private keys are hardened children of the device's signing private key---one device cannot derive the datastore public key of another device.  The device that wishes to share must somehow broadcast an attestation from its signing key that the datastore public key is specific to the given application.  Maybe this can go into the name's profile?

* Various data and storage methods need to be cleaned up to take a set of public keys to use to attempt to verify data.

issue_title: Look into eliminating the fabric_task decorator
issue_body:
_From @hannes-ucsc on April 13, 2015 2:44_

The `@fabric_task` decorator is inconvenient to use. One should be able to call Fabric functions in a box method without having to decorate that method with `@fabric_task`. That decorator's ability to temporarily change the remote user introduces considerable complexity since the decorator needs to maintain the previously set remote user such that it can revert to it when the decorated method returns. The same thing can be achieved with Fabric's `settings` context manager. 

We should be able invoke run(), sudo(), put(), get() by simply setting env.host and env.user. Switching the user should be done using `with settings( user=... )`. 

_Copied from original issue: BD2KGenomics/cgcloud-core#11_

Pyflakes was unable to detect undefined name in `__all__` when there exist undefined name outside of it. 

For example, given this code...

```python
from math import * # contain sin, cos, and csc
__all__ = ['sin', 'cos']
csc(1)
```

Pyflakes will report 

```
test.py:1: 'from math import *' used; unable to detect undefined names
test.py:5: 'csc' may be undefined, or defined from star imports: math

According to that, the code just have 2 "error", which is usage of `*` import and `csc` is undefined.
If I then fix the code 

```python
from math import csc
__all__ = ['sin', 'cos']
csc(1)
```

Then additional error shown up

```
test.py:3: undefined name 'cos' in __all__
test.py:3: undefined name 'sin' in __all__

Which then could finally fixed by

```python
from math import csc, cos, sin
__all__ = ['sin', 'cos']
csc(1)
```

issue_title: Oscar dynamic class loading break with recent Django configuration conventions
issue_body:
# Description

As noted in #2544 Django's recommended `INSTALLED_APPS` config is now `<appname>.apps.<AppnameConfig>`.

This completely breaks Oscar's dynamic class loading as Oscar expects the module name of a class being loaded to exactly match a module in `INSTALLED_APPS` ([ref.](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/core/loading.py#L97)).

This will become a significant issue as more and more people follow the newer, recommended way to configure Django `INSTALLED_APPS`.

oscar's is_quantity_allowed bases its answer on the basket's num_items before an update and the quantity in the proposed update. This is flawed because if you're updating the quantity for a line, it is double-counting the previous quantity for the line.

Steps to reproduce bug:
0) Set OSCAR_MAX_BASKET_QUANTITY_THRESHOLD in your settings to any number - say, 10.
1) add a single item to your cart.
2) update the quantity to be something less than the threshold, say 5
3) try to update the quantity to something more than threshold - current quantity - say, 6
Result:
Quantity: 6
Error message: "Due to technical limitations we are not able to ship more than 10 items in an order."

Relevant code: 


        if basket_threshold:
            total_basket_quantity = self.num_items
            max_allowed = basket_threshold - total_basket_quantity
            if qty > max_allowed:
                return False, _(
                    "Due to technical limitations we are not able "
                    "to ship more than %(threshold)d items in one order.") \
                    % {'threshold': basket_threshold}

in oscar/apps/basket/abstract_models.py

I have a question on customizing `LineAttributeSerializer`. When getting basket lines (`basket-line-detail`) I'd like to return option's code with `attributes`. Any help would be greatly appreciated.

**What tools I have tried with:**
1. Visual Studio 2017 v15.3
Using New Project, default templates, they don't get built because nuget packages are not restored
2. CLI
Tried to make new apps using commands, and the nuget package restore failed.
After that tried to use DotNet Restore, that returned the same results.

**What I have tried:**
1. Checked on CLI that DotNet --Version returns 2.0.0
2. Repaired Visual Studio.

currently the debian packages are version-specific.

for example, the current package is `dotnet-sdk-2.0.2`. this package will not upgrade automatically when a new version comes out. 

can we get a set of virtual packages `doetnet-sdk` , `dotnet-sdk-x`, and `dotnet-sdk-x.y` that automatically update? (and similar for the runtimes?)

for example, there's only one `openjdk-8-jdk` package. it installs the latest SDK, and it updates automatically. all packages that follow SemVer correctly can behave in this way without breaking existing clients.


EDIT: oh look at that, the docs i was looking at (https://www.microsoft.com/net/learn/get-started/linuxdebian) are out of date. the latest package is actually `dotnet-sdk-2.0.3`. i think this illustrates perfectly the need for non-version-specific packages.

We would  like some help understanding the functionality set by the JwtBearerOptions RequireHttpsMetadata boolean option.

[The documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.aspnetcore.authentication.jwtbearer.jwtbeareroptions.requirehttpsmetadata?view=aspnetcore-2.1) for this option is somewhat sparse, and I have been unable to find a more complete description.

While the documentation states that this should only be set to false in a development environment, all of our services including the authentication (bearer token issuing) service operate exclusively behind a reverse proxy gateway, within which environment they are HTTP not HTTPS. 

The name of the option suggests that the token issuer needs to be an HTTPS endpoint; however, in our scenario this will not be the case.

Given the significance of proper security configuration I would like to know exactly what effect this flag has, how it works and what the implications of leaving it false in our scenario are.

The **System.Runtime.CompilerServices.Unsafe** API seems to be missing the following:

	[MethodImpl(MethodImplOptions.AggressiveInlining)]
	public static IntPtr AsIntPtr<T>(ref T value) => (IntPtr)Unsafe.AsPointer(ref value);

The IL would be the same as the other `As...` functions:

    L_0000: ldarg.0
    L_0001: conv.u
    L_0002: ret

The objective is to displace the cast (from `void*` to `IntPtr`) out of end-user code scenarios which *otherwise wouldn't need to be marked as* `unsafe`. 

To restate, the use-case here is within certain "non-unsafe" (i.e. "safe?") C# methods which--if it weren't for calling to the existing `System.Runtime.CompilerServices.Unsafe.AsPointer(...)` method--would otherwise ***not*** need to be marked with the `unsafe` keyword. For example, transitonary code which needs to extract or expose the `IntPtr` and pass it down to other APIs, yet without itself ever needing to invoke `IntPtr.ToPointer()`.

This addition to the `Unsafe` library would allow such methods to obtain the `IntPtr` and still retain their "non-unsafe" status.

Although the C# shim shown above can (obviously) can be used to mitigate this omission, for completeness it would be nice to organize this function with its related kin by including it in `System.Runtime.CompilerServices.Unsafe`.

In Gentoo, Define's are passed by the initscript as -D arguments read in /etc/conf.d/apache

LE seems to ignore that. As a result, since the "Listen 443" directive is inside IfDefine blocks, it is systematically overlooked by LE since it doesn't know about the active Define directives.

LE will therefore add a "Listen 443" temporary directive, which will cause apache to fail with a "could not bind to address 0.0.0.0:443" error. LE in turn will fail with "urn:acme:error:connection" since apache is not running during the challenge.

When plotting markers geocoded from things like UK postcodes, it's quite common to get collisions when placing markers (multiple markers at the same location).

The markercluster approach *will* separate out markers that are colocated, but the overall effect can be quite unsatisfactory because the clustering algorithm tries to cluster things all over the place.

One solution is to perturb markers randomly. For example:

```
import numpy as np

m=folium.Map(location=loc, zoom_start=12)
for i in range(5):
    loc[0]=loc[0]+np.random.uniform(0.001, 10**(-20))-0.00005
    loc[1]=loc[1]+np.random.uniform(0.001, 10**(-20))-0.00005
    folium.Marker(loc, popup='{}'.format( i )).add_to(m)
m
```
What would be really useful would be a plugin to separate out colocated markers. [jawj/OverlappingMarkerSpiderfier-Leaflet](https://github.com/jawj/OverlappingMarkerSpiderfier-Leaflet) looks like it might be a useful candidate?

#### Expected Output

*Separate out colocated markers* but  __not__ *cluster markers AND separate out colocated ones*.

issue_title: Remove MarshallingError and UnmarshallingError in favor of a single ValidationError
issue_body:
Currently, `MarshallingError` and `UnmarshallingError` signal that an error should be stored in the `errors` dictionary during marshalling and unmarshalling. 

These exceptions only served a purpose prior to commit https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/00a18667b5a7a6a630d07062693e50007eefa5c8, when all exceptions were caught and reraised as (Un)MarshallingErrors. Now, they are only raised when a validation error occurs.

I propose to remove these exception classes in favor of a single `ValidationError`. Doing so would reduce the API and make accessing error messages when using `strict` mode much cleaner.

**Current**:

```python
from marshmallow.exceptions import MarshallingError, UnmarshallingError, ValidationError

schema = MySchema(strict=True)

try:
    schema.dump(some_obj)
except MarshallingError as err:
    if isinstance(err.underlying_error, ValidationError):
        messages = err.underlying_error.messages
    # ...

try:
    schema.load(some_obj)
except UnmarshallingError as err:
    if isinstance(err.underlying_error, ValidationError):
        messages = err.underlying_error.messages
    # ...
```

**Proposed**:

```python
from marshmallow.exceptions import ValidationError

schema = MySchema(strict=True)

try:
    schema.dump(some_obj)
except ValidationError as err:
    messages = err.messages
    # ...

try:
    schema.load(some_obj)
except ValidationError as err:
    messages = err.messages
    # ...
```

This could be implemented in conjunction with https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/128 .

**WARNING**: This would be a major breaking change for people using `strict` mode.

This is very similar to [the other bug I reported](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/64).  Turns out None renders strangely even when there's no ```many=True``` option specified.

```
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, pprint

class ChildSchema(Schema):
	name = fields.String()

class ParentSchema(Schema):
	child = fields.Nested(ChildSchema)

schema = ParentSchema()
result = schema.dump(object())
pprint(result.data)
```

Output:
```
{u'child': {u'name': u''}}
```

Expected:
```
{u'child': None}
```

Ideally, we should provide a way to create and manipulate plot objects in the API, which can then be rendered to Jupyter Notebook (see http://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/config/integrating.html#rich-display )

The resulting plot object could also be:
* shown in native GUI (`.show()`)
* saved to a file (`.save()`)
* combined with other plot objects into a tiled view (perhaps `tc.show(a, b, c, d)` where a,b,c,d are plot objects)

Mythril appears to miss the MUL overflow in [BECToken](https://etherscan.io/address/0xc5d105e63711398af9bbff092d4b6769c82f793d#code), a.k.a. batchOverflow. The overflows is located in the function `batchTransfer`, line 257. 

```
    uint256 amount = uint256(cnt) * _value;
```

Running the following fall to detect this issue:

```
$ myth -x BECToken.sol:BecToken
$ myth -x BECToken.sol:PausableToken
$ myth -x BECToken.sol:BecToken --max-depth 16
```

I also noticed that the runtime of the integer overflow tests increases heavily if --max-depth is increased. We might have to play with the `max_depth` setting of `search_children`.

@JoranHonig  can you look into this?

issue_title: Learning to rank using explicit preferences / rankings?
issue_body:
Hi @maciejkula , thank you for your fantastic library and examples as I have found them really helping in learning about implicit recommender systems. I just have a few questions that you can hopefully weigh in on.

I am an undergraduate CS student currently working on a project which involves eliciting user preferences using a game with purpose. (quite similar to the game described in this [paper by Hacker and Von Ahn](https://dl.acm.org/citation.cfm?id=1518882))
In short users are paired up and asked to indicate which of two movies (gathered from the MovieLens dataset) they would prefer while their partner is tasked with predicting their decision, and both players take turns expressing preferences and making predictions.
I have started to look at implementing a recommender system (based on the RelativeSVD algorithm described in the paper above) which works on data gathered from the game and have built one in python which performs gradient descent using (i,j,k) triplets indicating user i preferred item i to item j in the game and therefore trying to ensure item j is rated higher than item k in the (num_users x num_items) predicted score matrix.

I came across LightFM and noticed some similarities in this methodology and the implicit recommender built into LightFM which makes use of BPR and WARP loss, and would like to build and evaluate a LightFM model to work on the game data for comparison.
1. Would it be possible to adapt the LightFM model to work on explicit (positive_item, negative_item) pairings? I assume this would require changes to the underlying Cython responsible for implementing the BPR and WARP loss functions which may be non trivial. I also came across Spotlight when researching this which may offer a [slightly more friendly way of customizing the sampling](https://github.com/maciejkula/spotlight/blob/master/spotlight/factorization/implicit.py#L258) if I wanted to attempt to implement this myself?

Another idea I'm experimenting with is having users build up personal rankings of movies and having a reccomender work on these. For example imagine we have 10 movies with ids 0-9 and the following partial ordering for a user: `[1,4,5,6,8]` . Movie 1 has 4 movies ranked below it so it gets a value of 4, movie 4 has 3 below so it's value is 3 etc. down to movie 8 with a score of 0 (along with all other unordered movies)
Ideally I would like movies with higher values to appear higher up in the predicted scores and I'm using a `ndcg@k` evaulation to check this (with relevance being a scaled version of each movies interaction value)

2. Will LightFM work well with these sort of values in the interaction matrix? I notice the [linked example which works on sketchfab models](http://blog.ethanrosenthal.com/2016/11/07/implicit-mf-part-2/) treats interactions as likes which would be somewhat similar however a lot of the current evaluation seems to be focused on positive examples being non-zero and negative examples being zero (e.g binary interactions), but for this data it's important that a higher positive value is placed higher above smaller positive values. Based on issue #260 I believed that using `sample_weight` would improve things and yet from what I can see it makes no discernible affect on the outcome of training the model. Curiously, there seems to be no difference when using these positive interaction values which decrease alongside rank versus binary interactions with 1s indicating which items appear in the users partial ranking. This makes it difficult to optimize for `ndcg@k`. I'm wondering if perhaps I'm using the `sample_weight` argument incorrectly here? For the small example above movies `[1,4,5,6,8]` would have an interaction value of 1 and weights of `[4,3,2,1,0]` respectively. The idea being that ideally we would like to preserve as much of the original partial ranking in our predictions while generating a total ranking with which to make recommendations.

Apologies for the long post, any advice or suggestions with how to handle this data using LightFM (or another library if more suitable for the task) would be appreciated. If anything is unclear feel free to ask and I'll be happy to clarify. Thanks!

issue_title: Resizing file on fuse file system hangs
issue_body:
hi,
thanks for mutagen!

adding a tag with EasyMP3 used to work fine but now takes forever with recent linux kernels.

in particular, [this line] (https://github.com/quodlibet/mutagen/blob/master/mutagen/_util.py#L680) causes python to call C lib's [memmove on the mmap] (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Modules/mmapmodule.c#L684), and this in turn causes the kernel enter [noninterruptible sleep] (http://stackoverflow.com/questions/223644/what-is-an-uninterruptable-process) for a long time, eg **15 minutes to tag 60 MB mp3 file!!!**

this is not a bug in mutagen, but it renders the library useless. a simple workaround would be to copy the file contents to a temporary file instead of moving them in-place.

this would make mutagen safer too, given that if the tagging process is interrupted, mutagen as it is now corrupts the original mp3 file.

you can find more info on this issue [here] (https://github.com/Miserlou/SoundScrape/issues/101).

thank you.

issue_title: Complex Memory Leak in Theory of FP with Multiple check-sat Commands
issue_body:
On the `master` branch at commit bd187e098988c57b5dc8e75480c42717ffeb3482, consider the following input:

```
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun x0 () (_ FloatingPoint 2 3))
(check-sat)
(assert (= (fp.isSubnormal (fp.sqrt RTP (fp.max (fp.sqrt RNE (fp.sqrt RTZ (fp.neg (fp.add RTP x0 (fp.neg (fp.abs (fp.mul RTP x0 x0))))))) x0))) true))
(check-sat)
(check-sat)
```

Placing this into a file `test.smtlib2` and running with command-line `z3 -smt2 test.smtlib2` yields the following output:

```
sat
sat
sat
ast_manager LEAKED: 3
Leaked: decl z3.sk.0 :: bv[1]
id: 2147484139
Leaked: decl z3.sk.1 :: bv[1]
id: 2147484140
Leaked: bv
```

All the `(check-sat)` commands are necessary.  For as large as this input is, this is actually post-minimization with an experimental delta debugger.  If this can be further minimized, let me know.

issue_title: Request for help with int2bv / bv2int and possible bug (???)
issue_body:
Hi,
I have [this SO post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/48317925/z3-converting-from-bit-vectors-to-integers) which summarizes some of the questions regarding **int2bv** and **bv2int**.
It seems that finding a simple example where int2bv and/or bv2int fail
to behave as the semantics implied from their name is not that easy.
For example, the following query:

    (declare-const s String)
    (declare-const someBitVec3    (_ BitVec 3))
    (declare-const anotherBitVec3 (_ BitVec 3))
    (declare-const someInt                   Int)
    (declare-const anotherInt                Int)
    
    (assert (= (str.len s) (+ someInt anotherInt)))
    
    (assert (= (bv2int someBitVec3   ) someInt   ))
    (assert (= (bv2int anotherBitVec3) anotherInt))
    
    (assert (= (str.substr  s  0  6) "abcdef"))
    (assert (= (str.indexof s "#" 0) someInt))
    (assert (= (str.indexof s "$" 0) anotherInt))
    
    (assert (bvslt someBitVec3 anotherBitVec3))
    
    (check-sat)
    (get-value (s someInt anotherInt someBitVec3 anotherBitVec3))

Returns the expected answer:

    sat
    ((s "abcdef#$\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00")
     (someInt 6)
     (anotherInt 7)
     (someBitVec10 #b110)
     (anotherBitVec10 #b111))

Then I try to fail bv2int and force an overflow with this query,
but it really looks like I've hit a bug (???):

    (declare-const s String)
    (declare-const someBitVec3    (_ BitVec 3))
    (declare-const anotherBitVec3 (_ BitVec 3))
    (declare-const someInt                   Int)
    (declare-const anotherInt                Int)
    
    (assert (= (str.len s) (+ someInt anotherInt)))
    
    (assert (= (bv2int someBitVec3   ) someInt   ))
    (assert (= (bv2int anotherBitVec3) anotherInt))
    ;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;; change abcdef to abcdefg
    (assert (= (str.substr  s  0   7) "abcdefg"))
    (assert (= (str.indexof s "#" 0) someInt))
    (assert (= (str.indexof s "$" 0) anotherInt))
    
    (assert (bvslt someBitVec3 anotherBitVec3))
    
    (check-sat)
    (get-value (s someInt anotherInt someBitVec3 anotherBitVec3))
    
And the **weird** result is:

    sat
    ((s "\x00$\x00\x00#")
     (someInt 4)
     (anotherInt 1)
     (someBitVec3 #b100)
     (anotherBitVec3 #b001))

issue_title: Java API: Fatal Error While Accessing Optimizer Check()
Proposal for finite sorts in smt2 fixedpoint syntax extensions
issue_body:
I propose to have the Z3 fixedpoint engine support finite sorts (enums) in the SMT-LIB2 syntax, in addition to the bddbddb syntax.  Currently, we need to encode enums as bitvector constants.  This makes reading and writing the files unintuitive, and makes interpreting the answers involve an extra step for the user, where they must maintain their own mapping of enum name to bitvector constant.

According to the [tutorial](http://rise4fun.com/Z3/tutorialcontent/fixedpoints), the fixedpoint extensions to SMT-LIB2 include declare-{var,rule}, rule, query, and set-predicate-representation.  The simple

    (declare-datatypes T () (CONSTRUCTOR*))

doesn't work.  The declare-datatypes declaration is rather complicated, involving multiple types, constructors with different arities, and type parameters.  Moreover, it doesn't seem obvious how to tell when the user wants to use it as a finite type in a datalog program.  Thus, I think it would be prudent to have a completely separate concept for datalog finite types.  

How about this?

    (declare-finite-sort T (CONSTRUCTOR*))

I figure this would translate to the `datalog::context::symbol_sort_domain`.

Currently, pgen uses the function _set_output_dir to calculate the relative ".." identifiers needed to get from a tool project file to the actual project file referenced in the yaml. It adds this "count" to a dictionary that will eventually get passed to tools, and all tools must  call a _fix_paths function to append these ".."s to all filenames. I think this could be simplified using python's os.path.relpath function. 

I think it is in our best interest to simplify these dictionaries that are passed around as much as possible.

You can see how it is currently calculated here:
https://github.com/project-generator/project_generator/blob/dev_v0.7/project_generator/project.py#L391

And how it is used here:
https://github.com/project-generator/project_generator/blob/dev_v0.7/project_generator/tools/uvision.py#L123

You can see an examples of os.path.relpath below:
![relpaths](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/12565767/8655983/71e0258c-295a-11e5-9642-d5bf4363d59f.png)

issue_body:

issue_title: CFGFast() - networkx DiGraph nodes are missing predecessors
issue_body:
Hey guys,

I stumbled today upon some weirdness in the CFGFast()-analyses and @zardus  recommended me to open this issue.
In short, when using analyses.CFGFast() for generating a CFG, the networkx-digraph nodes appear to have at maximum one predecessor. At the same time, nodes can still have more than one successor, i.e. two successors, which is expected behavior but doesn't add up considering the observed amount of predecessors.

Here are some lines demonstrating the problem:
 `
import angr

b = angr.Project("/bin/true",load_options={'auto_load_libs': False})

cfg = b.analyses.CFGAccurate() #this yields expected results, i.e. nodes with more than one predeccors
#cfg = b.analyses.CFGFast()

for n in cfg.graph.nodes_iter():
    pre = n.predecessors #n.predecessors and cfg.graph.predecessors(n) are yielding the same result
    print "#Predecessors for %s: %d" % (n.name, len(pre)) 
    
for n in cfg.graph.nodes_iter():
    suc = n.successors #n.successors and cfg.graph.successors(n) are yielding the same result
    print "#Successors   for %s: %d" % (n.name, len(suc))
`

issue_title: Extremely slow performance for large job submission (XML and Dataset related)
issue_body:
After running some profiling and benchmarking I've discovered that a large source of performance problems is in parsing the XML for a job.

In order to reproduce this I was attempting to run over a 'standard' LHCb dataset of 8082 LFN with a standard LHCb job that effectively just counted the integrated luminosity for each lfn.

This job would stall for a few minutes at a time which didn't make much sense as I don't see this sort of performance bottleneck in simple profiling.

This turned up too late to be considered for 6.1.14, although I wanted to track down and identify what was causing this performance problem before 6.1.14 was released to know if this was a deal breaker.

The problem that I see is that constructing 8k DiracFile objects takes approximately 40s to perform and storing this on disk as a flat array of DiracFiles causes ganga to pause for the 2-3minutes required to process the xml file.

I think for 6.1.15 I would like to rewrite the internals of LHCbDataset to store the LFN in a flat array of strings and act effectively as a generator producing the DiracFile on demand.

Given that there is now a generic GangaDataset I would like to add this generator type functionality there and have a GangaDataset class which is able to store 'flat' information and generate IGangaFile objects on demand which would be more sensible than simply generating 8k (or more) DiracFile objects and storing the full object on disk in a huge XML.

(This could prove tricky with iterators and such but should also reduce the memory footprint which is a bit higher than I would like it to be atm ~200Mb per job when submitting which can expand when the dataset is copied/modified.)

While doing the work on atexit and (by extension) the coverage reporting (see #510) I've found a definite hard lock up. It is probably made worse by the coverage now working properly and I've only seen it on the CI machines but I can certainly see the problem.

Basically, when you set a value on a `GangaObject` through `__set__` it goes through `_getWriteAccess` which in turn locks the repository, i.e. Obj Lock -> Reg Lock. Flushing however, goes the other way, i.e. Reg lock (in `_flush`) -> Obj Lock. If the timing's bad you get a lock up.

I would argue that `_getWriteAccess` isn't needed when you set a value on an object as if that object is being flushed, either the flusher or the setter has the object lock and the other one has to wait. 

This could also be the source of the other lockups I saw with the coverage but I didn't check the stack traces for those.

What do people think?

Attrib Err: 'int' object has no attribute 'splitlines'
Attrib Err: 'bool' object has no attribute 'splitlines
```

This is from the vanilla develop branch running on 6.1.20 for the first time. I don't think this is too harmless but it does also raise some concern from me that something has gone un-noticed.

(For completeness i was launching 6.1.20 for the first time by running a script rather than dropping into the IPython prompt as I know this can cause different code paths to be executed)

issue_title: The Github oauth tutorial is not OpenID Connect compliant.
issue_body:
As @jmandel described in [his comment on the tutorial](http://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tutorials/authentication-github.html#comment-2568178329)

Using Github Bearer Token to authenticate directly to Kinto without any specific scoping of the Bearer Token means that any application having a Github Bearer Token for the user can manage, read, add and delete her Kinto data. This is not acceptable for production use.

This remembers me a discussion we had a month ago in London with @michielbdejong where he explained me that RemoteStorage embedded an OpenID Connect compatible server in charge of generating RemoteStorage scoped specific Bearer Token.

How should we handle this for Kinto, what is the plan forward to have a better OAuth2 integration.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Accounts API shouldn't commit to a specific authentication mechanism
issue_body:
Currently, the Accounts API is a wrapper around and replacement for the "basic auth" authentication mechanism. It allows us to detect a failed basic authenication as well as to update credentials for a given basic authentication without having to update the permissions on every object in the database. However, because an authentication mechanism is "baked in" to the accounts API, these benefits do not apply in other cases, such as when someone using the Portier mechanism changes their email address, or when someone wants to change their authentication mechanism from Portier to FXA or vice versa.

To address this, we should allow an account to be created with any authentication mechanism rather than just a username and password.

issue_title: Langpacks copied via pulp-admin on command line
issue_body:
You can copy langpacks between repositories. You define it with pulp-admin such as:

pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id repo_1

pulp-admin rpm repo uploads langpacks -i gorilla-%s -n gorilla-en -i zebra-%s -n zebra-docs --repo-id repo_1
You can verify it's added to the repo with:

pulp-admin rpm repo list --repo-id  repo_1
You can verify it's defined correctly with:
pulp-admin rpm repo content langpacks --repo-id repo_1

Create your second repo

pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id=repo_2

Copy the contents of first repo to the second repo

pulp-admin rpm repo copy langpacks --from-repo-id=repo_1 --to-repo-id=repo_2

Verify  that repo_2 contains newly copied package langpacks:

 pulp-admin repo list --repo-id repo_2


The feature story: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1367
Pulp's pulp-admin langpacks documentation

issue_title: Incorrect IPv6 and UDP header length fields in decompressed packets
issue_body:
When lowpan frames are decompressed, the constructed IPv6 and UDP header length fields include the appended CCM tag (length fields are larger by `sizeof(tag)`). In practice this is not a problem, because these raw values are not used, instead `message.GetLength()` is called to determine the payload size and it is updated correctly in `Mle::HandleUdpReceive` after the CCM decryption `SuccessOrExit(aMessage.SetLength(aMessage.GetLength() - sizeof(messageTag)));`. However this was quite confusing when I first dumped inbound packets on the host side and the buffer length did not match the header length field:

```
wpantund[24123]: [NCP->] CMD_PROP_VALUE_IS(PROP_STREAM_NET_INSECURE)           
wpantund[24123]: [NCP->] IPv6 len:87 type:17(cksum 0xd74d) [INSECURE] [DROPPED]
60 00 00 00 00 33 11 ff    fe 80 00 00 00 00 00 00                             
7e f4 85 67 ae f4 56 55    ff 02 00 00 00 00 00 00                             
00 00 00 00 00 00 00 01    4d 4c 4d 4c 00 33 d7 4d                             
00 15 a0 01 00 00 00 00    00 00 01 04 00 02 90 00                             
0b 08 38 3e d6 fd 00 02    7e 24 09 0b cd 00 00 00                             
40 08 00 00 00 f1 01  
```

This is probably not so important, as long as these frames are processed internally only.

issue_title: ncp: openthread-config.h.in assumes autotools and isn't toolchain portable
issue_body: The iar port of the openthread apps isn't able to find openthread-config.h since it is assumed to be autogenerated by autotools.  This will be a roadblock for building NCP firmware on other toolchains as well.

While running testing I encountered a crash here:

```
07 ffffb080`5c626bc8 fffff806`ddec221a nt!strnlen+0x7
08 ffffb080`5c626bd0 fffff806`ddec1b39 otlwf!Thread::MeshCoP::VendorNameTlv::SetVendorName+0x3a 
09 ffffb080`5c626c30 fffff806`ddec13bf otlwf!Thread::MeshCoP::Joiner::SendJoinerFinalize+0x1a9 
0a ffffb080`5c626e90 fffff806`ddec140c otlwf!Thread::MeshCoP::Joiner::HandleSecureCoapClientConnect+0x3f 
0b ffffb080`5c626ed0 fffff806`dded2318 otlwf!Thread::MeshCoP::Joiner::HandleSecureCoapClientConnect+0x1c 
0c ffffb080`5c626f00 fffff806`dded233c otlwf!Thread::Coap::SecureClient::HandleDtlsConnected+0x38 
0d ffffb080`5c626f30 fffff806`dded1857 otlwf!Thread::Coap::SecureClient::HandleDtlsConnected+0x1c 
0e ffffb080`5c626f60 fffff806`dded1ad7 otlwf!Thread::MeshCoP::Dtls::Process+0xa7 
0f ffffb080`5c6272c0 fffff806`dded2b58 otlwf!Thread::MeshCoP::Dtls::Receive+0x57 
```

This is because the `Joiner` class was holding onto the original pointers for the Vendor info passed into `Joiner::Start`. On Windows that was pointing back to memory that had already been freed by the system.

```
    mVendorName = aVendorName;
    mVendorModel = aVendorModel;
    mVendorSwVersion = aVendorSwVersion;
    mVendorData = aVendorData;
```

This actually crashes wpantund, but has roots in the mis-behaving remote device.

Not sure where this should be filed, as it is an architectural decision.

To reproduce:

From the 'wpanctl' application, use the "scan" command to scan for networks.  The code in wpantund (dbus, specifically) assumes, rightly or wrongly, that the network name is a UTF8 string. 

the NCP transmits a beacon request.

In this scenario, the remote device answering the beacon request has a bug and is sending a bad or invalid string as the network name. That device should transmit: "OpenThread" - instead it transmits: "O\377\377\377\377hread" (Where: \377 = Octal for an 0xFF byte).  It looks like something wrote a 32bit "-1" value over the top of something it should not have.

This goes over the air with the "0xff" bytes in place of a portion of the network name, up through the NCP and finally into Wpantund - where an assertion triggers this error:
```
process 592: arguments to dbus_message_iter_append_basic() were incorrect, assertion "_dbus_check_is_valid_utf8 (*string_p)" failed in file ../../dbus/dbus-message.c line 2676.
```
of course this should be fixed in the transmitting device, but the robustness principle states either the NCP, or WPANTUND ... something, somewhere shall not die a horrible death as it is right now.


-Duane.

issue_title: Consider alternative readline imports for OS compatibility
issue_body:
Mac OS X ships with libedit instead of libreadline.  libedit doesn't work well with Python's readline module.  So a user either needs to use homebrew to install GNU readline or they need to use pip to install the gnureadline module which is a version of Python's readline module which is statically linked to the GNU readline library.

So to help with Mac OS X compatibility for users that have gnureadline installed instead of libreadline, it would be good to have an import setup like so:

```Python
try:
    import gnureadline as readline
except ImportError:
    import readline
```

issue_title: [Proposal] Generate API objects from swagger spec
issue_body:
After using this library for a while, I can see a few areas in which the flow for authoring specs could be improved. Here's a sketch of a design, to get a conversation going, and to see if you guys have done any work in this area.

The general idea I'd like to propose is to load object definitions from the Swagger spec files that the k8s API exposes, and use those object definitions to generate the set of classes that can be used to build specs.

A couple options:
1) There are dynamic libraries like https://github.com/Yelp/bravado which load a Swagger spec into memory.
2) Something like swagger-codegen could be used to statically compile a client, which would then give IDE autocompetion. (I favour this option).

Now you can write code like

```
my_pod = Pod(
  containers=[
    Container(name='first', image='alpine'),
  ],
  ..
)
```

With each of these objects having all fields defined by the swagger spec. Tab completion for your Kubernetes specs!

The other benefit of this approach is that you no longer need to add classes manually for each Kind, which implies less work to support a new major API version. There are some questions here though; would the static client definitions be checked in to the codebase (giving completions out of the box, without any additional build steps), or loaded by the library user so that they have the correct definitions for their cluster (this would be required to add dynamically added third-party-resources). Either way it's simple to generate the client; you can run `swagger-codegen generate -i http://kubernetes.io/kubernetes/swagger-spec/ -l python -o k8s-swagger ` to load the public swagger definition and generate a fresh client implementation. https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen#homebrew has more detailed instructions if you want to play further.

I'd be interested in your thoughts on this, before I go too much further in exploring the implementation.

issue_title: Can you request a single cert with a wildcard and the base domain with our DNS plugins
issue_body:
There was some concern from @joohoi about whether or not we currently handle this correctly in any/all of our DNS plugins. @joohoi, if you could add some additional context here that'd be great. 

We should look into this issue soon as we're currently in the process of getting our DNS plugins packaged everywhere and we should try to avoid making distros immediately repackage them.
Create a sendmail configurator.

Gentoo provide a feature called sets. Letsencrypt  could use it to put dependency in.
This patch will create a set named letsencrypt-prerequisites with letsencrypt dep. And install it.

In this way it's easy  to find waht package was installed by letsencrypt.

```
diff --git a/bootstrap/_gentoo_common.sh b/bootstrap/_gentoo_common.sh
index f49dc00..d1c0e47 100755
--- a/bootstrap/_gentoo_common.sh
+++ b/bootstrap/_gentoo_common.sh
@@ -1,14 +1,13 @@
 #!/bin/sh
 
-PACKAGES="
-  dev-lang/python:2.7
-  dev-python/virtualenv
-  dev-util/dialog
-  app-admin/augeas
-  dev-libs/openssl
-  dev-libs/libffi
-  app-misc/ca-certificates
-  virtual/pkgconfig"
+PACKAGES="dev-lang/python:2.7
+dev-python/virtualenv
+dev-util/dialog
+app-admin/augeas
+dev-libs/openssl
+dev-libs/libffi
+app-misc/ca-certificates
+virtual/pkgconfig"
 
 case "$PACKAGE_MANAGER" in
   (paludis)
@@ -18,6 +17,8 @@ case "$PACKAGE_MANAGER" in
     pmerge --noreplace $PACKAGES
     ;;
   (portage|*)
-    emerge --noreplace $PACKAGES
+    mkdir -p /etc/portage/sets/
+    echo -e "$PACKAGES" > /etc/portage/sets/letsencrypt-prerequisites
+    emerge --noreplace -p @letsencrypt-prerequisites
     ;;
 esac

```

PS : set documentation is here : https://wiki.gentoo.org/wiki//etc/portage/sets

thank you :-)

hello i would like to make a small bug report regarding the manual.

inside the text of the manual there is a passage:

> The --force-renew flag may be helpful for automating renewal; it causes the expiration time of the certificate(s) to be ignored when considering renewal, and attempts to renew each and every installed certificate regardless of its age. (This form is not appropriate to run daily because each certificate will be renewed every day, which will quickly run into the certificate authority rate limit.)


if continued to scroll down however, one can see that there is no "--force-renew" but only a "--force-renewal" parameter.

>   --force-renewal, --renew-by-default
>                         If a certificate already exists for the requested
>                         domains, renew it now, regardless of whether it is
>                         near expiry. (Often --keep-until-expiring is more
>                         appropriate). Also implies --expand. (default: False)

it confused at least me so much that i now use --renew-by-default instead of the other one(s). maybe someone wanna fix that. thank you for your attention.

issue_title: document how different display modes interact with force_interactive
issue_body: ... because `force_interactive` doesn't actually force interactivity in non-interactive mode (it just uses the default instead), which is unintuitive and isn't written down anywhere.

    sudo certbot certonly --standalone --non-interactive  --preferred-challenge http-01

issue_title: Fix sporadic Apache unit test failures
issue_body:
The Apache plugin has begun sporadically failing unit tests in Travis. I have only seen this occur during coverage tests. Here's 3 different logs of the problem: [log1](https://gist.github.com/5d6e16b74e1b7008d5a6), [log2](https://travis-ci.org/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/jobs/100924529), [log3](https://gist.github.com/c464d11fb30c56ffac2c).

These problems need to be fixed as it slows down development, confuses new contributors, and may be caused by larger problems with the plugin.

issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Ubuntu 14.04 LTS

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

`tox`

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

`tests/travis-integration.sh`

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

Integration tests shouldn't fail spuriously on documentation only PRs.

## Here is a ~~Certbot~~ Travis log showing the issue (if available):
###### Logs are stored in `/var/log/letsencrypt` by default. Feel free to redact domains, e-mail and IP addresses as you see fit.
[log.txt](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/files/815499/log.txt)

@pavel-paulau, could you please remove the following package from PyPI: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/3ab8c7d15dcda79f1a5cde4f39b06614/0.0.0.dev20151024. It's showing up, when querying for letsencrypt: https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=search&term=letsencrypt&submit=search

Unfortunately, all other letsenncrypt-nginx-\* packages, although already removed, are also visible in the results. Would you be able to talk to PyPI team to resolve this issue, @pavel-paulau?

issue_title: Field type incorrect in child class vs parent class
issue_body:
Sorry, don't have time to submit *exact* reproduction steps.

**Environment:**
I have an abstract class with a JSONField:
class A:
    json = JSONField()
    class Meta:
       abstract = True

A polymorphic base class, and a child class:
class Doc(Polymorphic..., A):
    ....
class ChildDoc(Doc):
   ....

**Expected behavior:**
Get an instance of ChildDoc, use json member as a JSONField.

**Actual behavior:**
When I get the child class, the type of "json" is unicode object. Accessed via the embedded parent_ptr, it's the correct (ish) type.

```python
In [52]: a = Doc.objects.all().first()
In [53]: a?
Type:           ChildDoc
In [54]: a.json?
Type:        unicode
In [55]: a.doc_ptr.json?
Type:        dict
```

The documentation doesn't mention any caveats like this.

We have had a few mysterious resource sharing issue that was solved by [switching from fork to spawn process starting mechanism](https://github.com/vatlab/SOS/blob/62ee6ec9c06ed50fac6e0115eb3d5ff357262620/sos/__main__.py#L251). This also helps

1. Debug the code because some various (e.g. verbosity) was shared and not correctly passed.
2. Windows compatibility because windows does not support spawn.

However, it is proven that the spawn method is a lot slower than fork. Although this 1 to 2 second difference is not noticeable for real-world applications, it is very obvious for testing (of small scripts) and for first-expressions of users. It should be better to switch back to fork under mac/linux at some point.

The suspect of the problem was the singleton `env` class because we are copying/sharing a singleton class across processes. Perhaps we can get rid of this global singleton class.

Octave is very similar to Matlab so we have added octave as supported language. It uses the [`octave_kernel`](https://github.com/Calysto/octave_kernel) and is linked to the `Matlab` language (in `setup.py`). New development of the language interface has allowed a language module to work with multiple kernels  ( #704). 

Therefore, we will need to 

1. Install octave and octave kernel and check installation instruction on sos homepage. (Running SoS)
2. Check if octave kernel can be used by itself.
3. Check if octave can be used in SoS (with kernel `octave_kernel` and language `Matlab`)
4. If not work, check what is needed to make it work.
5. if works, add tests for matlab/octave under `test/Matlab` with tests similar to `R`.
6. After tests work, modify `.travis.yml` to install octave and octave_kernel, and test the octave kernel.

issue_title: resume task fails on non-submitting machine.
issue_body:
Currently resume is basically a resubmission of job, which generates job, transfer to remote host, and start. This make it impossible to resume a task from non-submitting machine on which the original task definition file `.def` does not exist. 

I suppose we can make resume a real resume in the sense that we simply resubmit the job on the remote host.

issue_title: Change of configuration file triggers rerun even if it has no impact on the pipeline
issue_body:
Here is a MWE. I have a script called `test.sos`:
```
[1]
output: CONFIG['input']
run:
  touch ${output}

[2]
input: CONFIG['input']
output: CONFIG['output']
task: workdir = './', concurrent = True
run:
  sleep 3
  touch ${_output}
```
and  I have two configuration files, `test1.conf`:

```
{'input':[ 'a2.out', 'a1.out'], 'output': ['b1.out', 'b2.out']}
```

and `test2.conf`:

```
{'input':[ 'a2.out'], 'output': ['b1.out']}
```

You see `test2.conf` specifies a subset of input/output in `test1.conf`. So one would imaging if I run first with `test1.conf` and later with `test2.conf` then the 2nd command should use signature from the first command instead of doing any computations. However it is not the case:

```
[GW] sos run test.sos -c test1.conf 
INFO: Executing default_1: 
INFO: default_1 input:    []
INFO: default_1 output:   ['a3.out', 'a2.out']
INFO: Executing default_2: 
INFO: default_2 input:    ['a3.out', 'a2.out']
INFO: default_2 output:   ['b1.out', 'b2.out']
INFO: Workflow default (ID=4c49155d99288f22) is executed successfully.
[GW] sos run test.sos -c test2.conf 
INFO: Executing default_1: 
INFO: default_1 input:    []
INFO: default_1 output:   ['a2.out']
INFO: Executing default_2: 
INFO: default_2 input:    ['a2.out']
INFO: default_2 output:   ['b1.out']
INFO: Workflow default (ID=4c49155d99288f22) is executed successfully.
```

In this case the behavior seems unreasonable. I expect CONFIG status is only relevant if it matters, but it seems we are checking the entire object to create the signature?

issue_title: Tests for new behavior of sos remove
issue_body:
* Remove signatures and rerun (existing)
* Remove targets (files) and rerun (new behavior)
* Zap targets and rerun
We have the `%toc` magic in the side panel but it could be useful to execute the `%toc` magic in the main notebook, which would create a toc in the main notebook, even in the exported HTML file.

Currently we run R action by `Rscript --defaultPackages=datasets,methods,utils,stats,grDevices,graphics`. This is to resolve problem with some systems that the startup environment does not load these packages with `Rscript`, even though the interactive R sessions do. However as complained by a colleague of mine, it turns out we are essentially customizing R start up environment by overriding the default, thus breaking the scripts for some R users.

So it seems to me that a compromise is to still use just `Rscript` to call the `R` action, but when we prepare R scripts we add to the beginning of it the following:

```
lapply(c('datasets','methods','utils','stats','grDevices','graphics'), require, character.only = TRUE)
```

That way we add in packages without overriding user's defaults. I'm just not sure what's the best place in SoS code to add in this line: as default `input=` option to `R` action prior to R scripts are generated? Or edit generated R scripts adding these in before execution?

issue_title: Allow Renamed Keyword Arguments to be Optional
issue_body:
#### The problem
When using the `rename` functionality to rename a keyword argument, an error is thrown if the keyword argument is not passed into the factory. For example, given a class with a field called `total_score` and adding `rename = {'score': 'total_score'}` will throw the following exception if `score` is not passed into the factory.

```
    @classmethod
    def _rename_fields(cls, **kwargs):
        for old_name, new_name in cls._meta.rename.items():
>           kwargs[new_name] = kwargs.pop(old_name)
E           KeyError: 'score'
```

#### Proposed solution
Allow the model instance to be created if the keyword argument is not present, essentially just ignoring the keyword conversion.

as seen in  #375 .
This line https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/tests/test_format.py#L288 does not work as many would expect.
``` FileNotFoundError ``` is not raised, if the directory exists (very much like Linux).

@peendebak  @eendebakpt  you can revert the last commit in the PR after I fix this.

The next version, and thus the master branch will ship without the multiprocessing code.
It's huge and we just don't want to keep maintaining it.

issue_title: PaginatedList breaks when trying to return a slice of elements greater than the max index
issue_body:
Consider the following standard Python list:
```
a = [1, 2, 3]
```

Attempting to call `a[4:5]` on it returns `[]`.

This does not happen for `PaginatedList` objects. Instead, `_grow()` runs forever, trying to access an index that doesn't exist.

Hi, I am interested in adding USPS Dataset for digit classification, which commonly used in domain adaption.

I find [this repo](https://github.com/mingyuliutw/CoGAN_PyTorch/blob/master/src/dataset_usps.py) already implement it, and I modify it slightly [here](https://github.com/corenel/torchzoo/blob/master/torchzoo/datasets/usps.py) referring to the format of MNIST dataset. Could I make a pull request to add it in torchvision ?

BTW, I write a class for MNIST-M dataset [here](https://github.com/corenel/torchzoo/blob/master/torchzoo/datasets/mnistm.py) which consists of MNIST digits blended with random color patches from the BSDS500 dataset. That's also frequently used in domain adaption. But its code is heavily overlapping the original MNIST dataset except the downloading and dataset-making process. I'm wondering whether it's appropriate to add it directly to torchvision, or how to modify it.

Look forward to your suggestions and guidance.

issue_title: Fine-tuning inception v3 - low learning rate

issue_body:
In [the save_image function](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/utils.py#L53), there's an offset added to images. Assuming that my image tensor has values between 0 and 1, my "pre-save" tensor will have values between 0.5 and 1, resulting in a final PIL image with values between 128 and 255.

This seems like a bug? I'd propose having no offset.
Thanks! :smile:

The `dependsOn` property for a resource currently takes an array. This is great for cases where you want multiple dependencies, but it's very common to just have a single dependency. We can change this to be of type `Resource | Resource[]`, and accept both, very easily.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.

- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**:  source

- **Python version**: 3.5

### Describe the problem
```
import ray

@ray.remote(num_cpus=1)

issue_title: ComponentFailureTest.testPlasmaManagerFailed test hangs frequently in Travis in MacOS/Python3 build.
issue_body:
The following test fails nearly every time in Travis on the MacOS/Python 3.5 build.

```
python test/component_failures_test.py ComponentFailureTest.testPlasmaManagerFailed
```

Note that the line https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/617bc4d2394aec607755a1a33f16271c38298f3f/test/component_failures_test.py#L134 has a bug, it should just be `num_workers_per_scheduler`. After making that change, I can reproduce the problem locally by running the test in a loop.

Investigating the bug a bit more, it appears that **somehow a task is assigned to a local scheduler that has been removed**. This shouldn't happen because the monitor should iterate through all of the tasks in the task table and clean them up. However, there may be a race condition.
