Please be sure to read through other issues as well to make sure what you are suggesting/reporting has not already
been suggested/reported

### Type:

- [x] Suggestion
- [ ] Bug

### Brief description of the problem
Currently, it's difficult to set a specific data folder for config. The use of self works fine in most cases, but it does not play well with multiple or single inheritance. If you try to do this, you will end up having multiple data folders, each having a different copy of the operations you have performed. See code in `steps to reproduce` It would be great if we could have another way of setting the main "data" folder for our cogs.
### Expected behavior
One centralized data folder.
### Actual behavior
Splits the data into separate folders.

### Steps to reproduce
The following hastebin links to code that shows the problem:    
https://hastebin.com/utetigekup.py

The work around provided by @Tobotimus uses a named tuple to bypass the issue.     
https://hastebin.com/favisiselo.py

# Other bugs

<!-- 
Did you find a bug with something other than a command? Fill out the following:
-->

#### What were you trying to do?
autorole setup
<!-- Replace this line with a description of what you were trying to do -->

#### What were you expecting to happen?
fix
<!-- Replace this line with a description of what you were expecting to happen -->

#### What actually happened?
erorr
<!-- Replace this line with a description of what actually happened. Include any error messages -->
https://pastebin.com/pbt2svh4
Error in command 'autorole add'. Check your console or logs for details.
#### How can we reproduce this issue?
you corrected
<!-- Replace with numbered steps to reproduce the issue -->

The `to_address()` method on the `P2pkhScript` returns a p2sh address, while the `address()` method returns the expected p2pkh address. This happens because the class itself does not implement the `to_address()` and only inherits it from `ScriptPubKey` where it is hardcoded to always give a p2sh if it's not segwit. I can see two ways to fix this, either in the parent or the child class:
In the parent class change the `address()` method to default to p2sh and change the `else` part in to_address to `return self.address()`. This preserves the old behaviour for child classes that do not implement `address()` but fixes it for those that do.
Or change the child class(es) to implement `to_address()`.

Below a minimal test case that shows the unexpected/surprising behaviour:

```import btcpy

from btcpy.structs.script import P2pkhScript
from btcpy.structs.crypto import PublicKey

btcpy.setup.setup('mainnet')
script = P2pkhScript(PublicKey.unhexlify('02f1a8ff45acaf2c4405436e44302fcf5e56c1c366e5ad2aebb148e8de4aa92341'))
print(script.to_address()) # 39GzTi9aMuVUwPX9DxxokygtLNvfBLzPhR
print(script.address()) # 1HXWXZovrY7rT7TFQq2dmd9knPFYAXa6VG
assert script.to_address() == script.address()
```

This is a placeholder while still working on the issue, I will add more items later, as well as a full example. This isn't meant to be exhaustive, but follows a somewhat complex (though not very long) Dockerfile to Singularity recipe conversion example.

- [ ] Dockerfile `USER` directives should be removed and a warning emitted (I assume, or is this possible in Singularity?).
- [ ] Quotes are currently not expanded as in a shell, so having something like this under `%files` will not work: `"./${DISTRO_INSTALL_CMDS}" /tmp" - in this particular case, just removing the double quotes will work.
- [ ] If destination directories do not exist under `%files`, a `mkdir -p` may need to be added under `%setup`.
- [ ] changes to `$HOME` should issue a warning. This is typically not going to be the same sort of thing in Singularity since by default `HOME` is bind mounted to the actual user's home directory, potentially resulting in damage tot he user's system. However, since `$HOME` doesn't exist during the build, I'm not sure what the exact behavior would be, but probably not the expected behavior in any case. Ideally, one might be able to specifying in the Singularity recipe to use a non-bind mounted $HOME, though I'm not sure this is possible.

issue_body:
I've managed to block in a nursery scope several times (eg "while True:" loops) while learning trio, not realizing I had not hit ```__aexit__```.  For long-running processes, this will appear to succeed as we don't expect to exit until KeyboardInterrupt.  However, this will have disabled all of the reaping machinery in ```__aexit__``` and will be silently accumulating zombie tasks indefinitely.

The documentation is fairly clear on this so I am hesitant to suggest the need to add idiot-proofing, but if there is an easy way to detect the nursery is running but not in ```__aexit__``` it might prevent some grief.

issue_title: Reduce danger of accidental exception suppression
issue_body:
Consider the following code. It consists of an echo server with a bug, and a unit test for the server:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import pytest
import pytest_trio
import trio
import logging

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
PORT = 12346
BUFSIZE = 16384
async def echo_server(server_sock):
    try:
        with server_sock:
            await server_sock.recv(BUFSIZE)
            raise RuntimeError('Damn!')
    except Exception as exc:
        await trio.sleep(0.1) # make problem deterministic
        log.exception('Things went wrong...')

async def echo_listener(nursery):
    with trio.socket.socket() as listen_sock:
        await listen_sock.bind(("127.0.0.1", PORT))
        listen_sock.listen()
        while True:
            server_sock, _ = await listen_sock.accept()
            nursery.start_soon(echo_server, server_sock)

async def parent():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(echo_listener, nursery)

@pytest.mark.trio
async def test_server(nursery):
    nursery.start_soon(parent)
    await trio.sleep(0.5) # wait for server to listen
    with trio.socket.socket() as client_sock:
        await client_sock.connect(("127.0.0.1", PORT))
        await client_sock.send(b'hello, world')
        resp = await client_sock.recv(BUFSIZE)
        assert resp == b'hello, world'
```

As expected, the unittest fails - but the root cause is nowhere to be seen:

```
$ python3 -m pytest tests/lograce.py 
==================================== test session starts =====================================
platform linux -- Python 3.6.4, pytest-3.3.2, py-1.5.2, pluggy-0.6.0 -- /home/nikratio/consulting/iofabric/venv/bin/python3
cachedir: tests/.cache
rootdir: /home/nikratio/consulting/iofabric/gruagach/tests, inifile: pytest.ini
plugins: trio-0.3.0
collected 1 item                                                                             

tests/lograce.py::test_server FAILED                                                   [100%]

========================================== FAILURES ==========================================
________________________________________ test_server _________________________________________
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/nikratio/consulting/iofabric/gruagach/tests/lograce.py", line 41, in test_server
    assert resp == b'hello, world'
AssertionError: assert b'' == b'hello, world'
  Right contains more items, first extra item: 104
  Full diff:
  - b''
  + b'hello, world'
================================== 1 failed in 0.63 seconds ==================================
```

If we force the test to wait a moment before failing...

```python
@pytest.mark.trio
async def test_server(nursery):
    nursery.start_soon(parent)
    await trio.sleep(0.5)  # wait for server to listen
    try:
        with trio.socket.socket() as client_sock:
            await client_sock.connect(("127.0.0.1", PORT))
            await client_sock.send(b'hello, world')
            resp = await client_sock.recv(BUFSIZE)
            assert resp == b'hello, world'
    finally:
        await trio.sleep(0.5)
```

...we get much better results:

```
$ python3 -m pytest tests/lograce.py 
[...]
collected 1 item                                                                             

tests/lograce.py::test_server FAILED                                                   [100%]

========================================== FAILURES ==========================================
________________________________________ test_server _________________________________________
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/nikratio/consulting/iofabric/gruagach/tests/lograce.py", line 42, in test_server
    assert resp == b'hello, world'
AssertionError: assert b'' == b'hello, world'
  Right contains more items, first extra item: 104
  Full diff:
  - b''
  + b'hello, world'
------------------------------------- Captured log call --------------------------------------
21:55:13.383 ERROR    lograce.echo_server: Things went wrong...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/nikratio/consulting/iofabric/gruagach/tests/lograce.py", line 15, in echo_server
    raise RuntimeError('Damn!')
RuntimeError: Damn!
================================== 1 failed in 1.13 seconds ==================================
```

I believe the problem is that the failing test causes trio to cancel the server tasks before it has a chance to log its exception. Note that the call to `trio.wait` in `echo_server` is not necessay for the problem to occur, it just makes it much more likely. 

I am not sure how to best fix this. Maybe the trio test runner could, if the test fails, give the other nursery task a short grace period in which they have a chance to report any errors before cancelling them?

This is a placeholder to track progress on feature development towards building Execution Providers.

Execution Providers are interfaces to compute resources such as clouds, clusters with schedulers, container orchestration systems etc. Such an interface would provide a reliable method to start tasks and most often pilot mechanisms that generally offer higher task dispatch rates.

As of this post we have the following:

- [X] Execution Provider interface 
- [X] Slurm provider, tested on (RCC/midway and Nersc/Cori)
- [X] AWS provider
- [X] Azure provider
- [x] Jetstream provider that partially complies.
- [x] PBS provider
- [x] Condor provider (@LincolnBryant)

Part of this work is being tracked on this [google doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/193LBq7H-dtxrYUER7oZqs0ZlOcLa2fGFPGLOHRS1c5U/edit?usp=sharing)

issue_title: HTTP GET S3 object always returns 403.
issue_body: Trying to get a file from a S3 bucket always results in a 403. I've set the bucket policy to allow getObject from * but I don't think localstack supports bucket policies anyway. Here is what I'm running. ``` curl -v http://localstack-core:4572/mybucket/index.html > GET /mybucket/index.html HTTP/1.1 > Host: localstack-core:4572 > User-Agent: curl/7.52.1 > Accept: */* > < HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden < Server: BaseHTTP/0.3 Python/2.7.13 < Date: Sun, 15 Oct 2017 20:26:20 GMT < Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8 < Content-Length: 0 < Server: Werkzeug/0.12.2 Python/2.7.13 < Date: Sun, 15 Oct 2017 20:26:20 GMT < Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * < Access-Control-Allow-Methods: HEAD,GET,PUT,POST,DELETE,OPTIONS,PATCH < Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization,content-type,content-md5,x-amz-content-sha256,x-amz-date,x-amz-security-token,x-amz-user-agent < * Curl_http_done: called premature == 0 * Connection #0 to host localstack-core left intact ``` If I try it on a file that doesn't exist. issue_title: Failing tests due to Pandas not supporting Python <3.5
issue_body:
Currently our tests for Python 3.4 fail due to Pandas only being available for Python 2.7, 3.5 and 3.6 ( [source](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/install.html#python-version-support)).

I propose we skip the Pandas tests for Python <3.5 as it is an optional dependency and the core of `sentinelsat` works fine with Python 3.3-3.6.

issue_body: localize the concept of data sets to certain sub packages

@jayvdb, as per our discussion, here is the story: 

Take environment variables into data configuration so that they are visible in templating operations.

issue_title: Usage of assert statements in optimized mode
issue_body:
I've noticed that Lark uses assert statements in some places outside of tests. Just by doing `git grep assert` in the lark directory we can see a big list of these.

Assert statements are ignored when Python is running in optimized mode. Optimize mode is used in some projects when Python is deployed and/or distributed. I did a quick experiment, and running the following like of code fails in both normal and optimized mode:

```
lark.Lark(grammar='start: "my_token"', parser='not_a_parser')
```

But the yielded exception is different. Normal mode is:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/lark.py", line 108, in __init__
    self.options = LarkOptions(options)
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/lark.py", line 68, in __init__
    assert self.parser in ('earley', 'lalr', 'cyk', None)
AssertionError
```

And optimized is:

```

    self.parser = self._build_parser()

    raise ValueError('Unknown parser: %s' % parser)
ValueError: Unknown parser: not_a_parser
```

This particular example might not be too problematic, but might the usage of asserts became problematic for people running lark in optimized mode?

issue_title: Authorization URI should be accessible via authorize_url method
issue_body:
```authorize_url``` is currently an attribute on the Client class. It should be accessible via a method call:

```python
client = soundcloud.Client(auth_token='foo')
client.authorize_url()
```

Instead of 

```python
client = soundcloud.Client(auth_token='foo')
client.authorize_url
```

In the interest of backwards compatibility and robustness, perhaps it should be both.
